“And how,” she said, turning the crystal dolphin to stand straight, “how did you enjoy the play last night?” “Oh, they had to go before the end!” she said. “They had to be back at ten!” she said. “So they don’t know what happened,” she said. “That does seem hard luck,” she said (for her servants stayed later, if they asked her). “That does seem rather a shame,” she said, taking the old bald-looking cushion in the middle of the sofa and putting it in Lucy’s arms, and giving her a little push, and crying:

“Take it away! Give it to Mrs. Walker with my compliments! Take it away!” she cried.
explanation: This passage presents Clarissa's spoken dialogue and actions through conventional third-person narration. While it reveals character, it does not render unspoken consciousness, making it omniscient description rather than SOC.
evidence: Reporting verbs ('she said', 'she cried') frame all speech, Actions described externally ('taking the... cushion', 'giving her a little push'), No representation of Clarissa's unspoken thoughts during this exchange
notes: This is dialogue and action, not stream of consciousness.

passage:
But, said Mrs. Dalloway, she had enough on her hands already, quite enough of her own to do without that.

“But, thank you, Lucy, oh, thank you,” said Mrs. Dalloway, and thank you, thank you, she went on saying (sitting down on the sofa with her dress over her knees, her scissors, her silks), thank you, thank you, she went on saying in gratitude to her servants generally for helping her to be like this, to be what she wanted, gentle, generous-hearted. Her servants liked her.
explanation: The narrator describes Clarissa's gratitude toward her servants using conventional third-person narration. The final sentence ('Her servants liked her') is pure narratorial assertion, confirming this as omniscient description.
evidence: Reporting verbs frame all speech and thought, Narrator summarizes Clarissa's motivation ('in gratitude to her servants generally'), Final sentence is external assessment ('Her servants liked her')

passage: And then this dress of hers--where was the tear? and now her needle to be threaded. This was a favourite dress, one of Sally Parker’s, the last almost she ever made, alas, for Sally had now retired, living at Ealing, and if ever I have a moment, thought Clarissa (but never would she have a moment any more), I shall go and see her at Ealing. For she was a character, thought Clarissa, a real artist. She thought of little out-of-the-way things; yet her dresses were never queer. You could wear them at Hatfield; at Buckingham Palace. She had worn them at Hatfield; at Buckingham Palace.
explanation: Clarissa's thoughts about her dressmaker Sally Parker blend practical concern with nostalgic reflection. The shift to first-person ('if ever I have a moment') within third-person narration is characteristic of Woolf's indirect interior monologue.
evidence: Intrusion of first-person thought within third-person frame, Parenthetical self-correction ('but never would she have a moment any more'), Associative movement from dress to dressmaker to social occasions

passage: Quiet descended on her, calm, content, as her needle, drawing the silk smoothly to its gentle pause, collected the green folds together and attached them, very lightly, to the belt. So on a summer’s day waves collect, overbalance, and fall; collect and fall; and the whole world seems to be saying “that is all” more and more ponderously, until even the heart in the body which lies in the sun on the beach says too, That is all. Fear no more, says the heart. Fear no more, says the heart, committing its burden to some sea, which sighs collectively for all sorrows, and renews, begins, collects, lets fall. And the body alone listens to the passing bee; the wave breaking; the dog barking, far away barking and barking.
explanation: Clarissa's meditative state while sewing is rendered through lyrical indirect interior monologue that simulates contentment through rhythmic syntax and oceanic imagery. The repetition and flowing cadence formally enact tranquility.
evidence: Wave metaphor extended through repetitive structure ('collect and fall'), Personification of heart saying 'Fear no more' as internalized comfort, Sensory details ('passing bee', 'dog barking') filtered through peaceful perception
notes: This passage exemplifies Steinberg's 'simulation of a state of mind'—the form itself enacts contentment.

passage:
“Heavens, the front-door bell!” exclaimed Clarissa, staying her needle. Roused, she listened.

“Mrs. Dalloway will see me,” said the elderly man in the hall. “Oh yes, she will see _me_,” he repeated, putting Lucy aside very benevolently, and running upstairs ever so quickly. “Yes, yes, yes,” he muttered as he ran upstairs. “She will see me. After five years in India, Clarissa will see me.”
explanation: The narrator describes Peter's thoughts and actions from outside, using conventional third-person narration with reporting verbs. This is omniscient description of consciousness rather than interior monologue.
evidence: Reporting verbs ('said', 'repeated', 'muttered') frame all speech, Narrator describes Peter's actions ('putting Lucy aside', 'running upstairs'), No direct access to Peter's unmediated thought processes

passage: “Who can--what can,” asked Mrs. Dalloway (thinking it was outrageous to be interrupted at eleven o’clock on the morning of the day she was giving a party), hearing a step on the stairs. She heard a hand upon the door. She made to hide her dress, like a virgin protecting chastity, respecting privacy. Now the brass knob slipped. Now the door opened, and in came--for a single second she could not remember what he was called! so surprised she was to see him, so glad, so shy, so utterly taken aback to have Peter Walsh come to her unexpectedly in the morning! (She had not read his letter.)
explanation: Clarissa's reaction to Peter's unexpected arrival is rendered through indirect interior monologue, with parenthetical thought, simile ('like a virgin'), and emotional descriptors capturing her flustered state.
evidence: Parenthetical intrusion of thought about interruption, Simile comparing dress-hiding to 'virgin protecting chastity', Emotional cascade ('so surprised... so glad, so shy, so utterly taken aback')

passage: “And how are you?” said Peter Walsh, positively trembling; taking both her hands; kissing both her hands. She’s grown older, he thought, sitting down. I shan’t tell her anything about it, he thought, for she’s grown older. She’s looking at me, he thought, a sudden embarrassment coming over him, though he had kissed her hands. Putting his hand into his pocket, he took out a large pocket-knife and half opened the blade. Exactly the same, thought Clarissa; the same queer look; the same check suit; a little out of the straight his face is, a little thinner, dryer, perhaps, but he looks awfully well, and just the same.
explanation: The passage alternates between Peter's and Clarissa's consciousness using indirect interior monologue, with minimal narratorial presence. The shift between perspectives is seamless, characteristic of Woolf's technique.
evidence: Peter's thoughts rendered through 'he thought' with character-specific concerns, Clarissa's observations rendered through 'thought Clarissa' with perceptual details, Shared focus on physical appearance and aging as emotional subtext
notes: This demonstrates space-montage by rapidly shifting between two characters' perspectives on the same encounter.

passage:
“How heavenly it is to see you again!” she exclaimed. He had his knife out. That’s so like him, she thought. He had only reached town last night, he said; would have to go down into the country at once; and how was everything, how was everybody--Richard? Elizabeth?

“And what’s all this?” he said, tilting his penknife towards her green dress. He’s very well dressed, thought Clarissa; yet he always criticises _me_. Here she is mending her dress; mending her dress as usual, he thought; here she’s been sitting all the time I’ve been in India; mending her dress; playing about; going to parties; running to the House and back and all that, he thought, growing more and more irritated, more and more agitated, for there’s nothing in the world so bad for some women as marriage, he thought; and politics; and having a Conservative husband, like the admirable Richard. So it is, so it is, he thought, shutting his knife with a snap.
explanation: The passage alternates between Clarissa's and Peter's consciousness, with Peter's increasingly agitated thoughts about Clarissa's life rendered through repetitive, judgmental phrasing that simulates his irritation.
evidence: Clarissa's brief thought ('That’s so like him') contrasting with Peter's extended critique, Peter's repetitive phrasing ('mending her dress; mending her dress as usual') showing fixation, Escalating judgment about marriage and politics revealing Peter's emotional state
notes: Peter's thoughts simulate irritation through repetitive, accusatory syntax.

passage:
“Richard’s very well. Richard’s at a Committee,” said Clarissa. And she opened her scissors, and said, did he mind her just finishing what she was doing to her dress, for they had a party that night?

“Which I shan’t ask you to,” she said. “My dear Peter!” she said. But it was delicious to hear her say that--my dear Peter! Indeed, it was all so delicious--the silver, the chairs; all so delicious! Why wouldn’t she ask him to her party? he asked. Now of course, thought Clarissa, he’s enchanting! perfectly enchanting! Now I remember how impossible it was ever to make up my mind--and why did I make up my mind--not to marry him? she wondered, that awful summer?
explanation: The passage alternates between Peter's and Clarissa's consciousness, with Clarissa's nostalgic questioning about her past decision rendered through indirect interior monologue with emotional emphasis.
evidence: Peter's italicized thought ('my dear Peter!') showing his pleasure, Clarissa's repetitive exclamation ('perfectly enchanting!') indicating emotional resurgence, Rhetorical question about past decision ('why did I make up my mind') revealing unresolved feelings

passage:
“But it’s so extraordinary that you should have come this morning!” she cried, putting her hands, one on top of another, down on her dress.

“Do you remember,” she said, “how the blinds used to flap at Bourton?”

“They did,” he said; and he remembered breakfasting alone, very awkwardly, with her father; who had died; and he had not written to Clarissa. But he had never got on well with old Parry, that querulous, weak-kneed old man, Clarissa’s father, Justin Parry.

“I often wish I’d got on better with your father,” he said.

“But he never liked any one who--our friends,” said Clarissa; and could have bitten her tongue for thus reminding Peter that he had wanted to marry her. Of course I did, thought Peter; it almost broke my heart too, he thought; and was overcome with his own grief, which rose like a moon looked at from a terrace, ghastly beautiful with light from the sunken day. I was more unhappy than I’ve ever been since, he thought. And as if in truth he were sitting there on the terrace he edged a little towards Clarissa; put his hand out; raised it; let it fall. There above them it hung, that moon. She too seemed to be sitting with him on the terrace, in the moonlight.
explanation: Peter's recollection of past grief is rendered through indirect interior monologue with powerful lunar imagery that simulates his emotional state. The passage shifts between characters' consciousness seamlessly.
evidence: Moon metaphor extended through 'ghastly beautiful with light from the sunken day', Physical gestures ('edged a little towards Clarissa') emerging from emotional memory, Shared imaginary setting ('She too seemed to be sitting with him') showing merged consciousness
notes: The passage simulates grief through the haunting, luminous quality of the moon imagery.

passage: “Herbert has it now,” she said. “I never go there now,” she said. Then, just as happens on a terrace in the moonlight, when one person begins to feel ashamed that he is already bored, and yet as the other sits silent, very quiet, sadly looking at the moon, does not like to speak, moves his foot, clears his throat, notices some iron scroll on a table leg, stirs a leaf, but says nothing--so Peter Walsh did now. For why go back like this to the past? he thought. Why make him think of it again? Why make him suffer, when she had tortured him so infernally? Why?
explanation: Peter's self-reproach for revisiting painful memories is rendered through indirect interior monologue, with repetitive questioning that simulates his guilt and emotional turmoil.
evidence: Extended simile comparing present moment to terrace scene, Repetitive self-questioning ('Why...? Why...? Why...? Why?') showing guilt, Accusatory self-description ('when she had tortured him so infernally')
notes: The passage simulates guilt through the escalating, self-accusatory questions.

passage: “Do you remember the lake?” she said, in an abrupt voice, under the pressure of an emotion which caught her heart, made the muscles of her throat stiff, and contracted her lips in a spasm as she said “lake.” For she was a child, throwing bread to the ducks, between her parents, and at the same time a grown woman coming to her parents who stood by the lake, holding her life in her arms which, as she neared them, grew larger and larger in her arms, until it became a whole life, a complete life, which she put down by them and said, “This is what I have made of it! This!” And what had she made of it? What, indeed? sitting there sewing this morning with Peter. She looked at Peter Walsh; her look, passing through all that time and that emotion, reached him doubtfully; settled on him tearfully; and rose and fluttered away, as a bird touches a branch and rises and flutters away. Quite simply she wiped her eyes.
explanation: Clarissa's existential questioning about her life's meaning is rendered through indirect interior monologue with powerful imagery of holding and presenting her life. The bird metaphor captures the fragility of her emotional connection.
evidence: Bodily description of emotion ('muscles of her throat stiff'), Metaphor of life as object held and presented ('holding her life in her arms'), Bird simile expressing tentative emotional connection ('touches a branch and rises')
notes: The passage simulates existential doubt through the contrast between the weight of 'whole life' and the fragility of the bird image.

passage: “Yes,” said Peter. “Yes, yes, yes,” he said, as if she drew up to the surface something which positively hurt him as it rose. Stop! Stop! he wanted to cry. For he was not old; his life was not over; not by any means. He was only just past fifty. Shall I tell her, he thought, or not? He would like to make a clean breast of it all. But she is too cold, he thought; sewing, with her scissors; Daisy would look ordinary beside Clarissa. And she would think me a failure, which I am in their sense, he thought; in the Dalloways’ sense. Oh yes, he had no doubt about that; he was a failure, compared with all this--the inlaid table, the mounted paper-knife, the dolphin and the candlesticks, the chair-covers and the old valuable English tinted prints--he was a failure! I detest the smugness of the whole affair he thought; Richard’s doing, not Clarissa’s; save that she married him.
explanation: Peter's insecure self-assessment is rendered through indirect interior monologue, with repetitive phrasing and catalog of domestic objects that simulate his feelings of inadequacy and resentment.
evidence: Repetitive affirmation ('Yes, yes, yes') masking pain, Internal debate ('Shall I tell her, he thought, or not?') showing hesitation, Catalog of domestic objects as symbols of perceived failure
notes: The passage simulates insecurity through the contrast between Peter's self-perception and the Dalloways' material success.

passage: And this has been going on all the time! he thought; week after week; Clarissa’s life; while I--he thought; and at once everything seemed to radiate from him; journeys; rides; quarrels; adventures; bridge parties; love affairs; work; work, work! and he took out his knife quite openly--his old horn-handled knife which Clarissa could swear he had had these thirty years--and clenched his fist upon it. What an extraordinary habit that was, Clarissa thought; always playing with a knife. Always making one feel, too, frivolous; empty-minded; a mere silly chatterbox, as he used. But I too, she thought, and, taking up her needle, summoned, like a Queen whose guards have fallen asleep and left her unprotected (she had been quite taken aback by this visit--it had upset her) so that any one can stroll in and have a look at her where she lies with the brambles curving over her, summoned to her help the things she did; the things she liked; her husband; Elizabeth; her self, in short, which Peter hardly knew now, all to come about her and beat off the enemy.
explanation: The passage alternates between Peter's defensive assertion of his active life and Clarissa's defensive summoning of her identity against his judgment. Both use indirect interior monologue with vivid metaphors.
evidence: Peter's catalog of experiences ('journeys; rides; quarrels...') as self-justification, Clarissa's royal metaphor ('like a Queen whose guards have fallen asleep'), Contrasting symbolic objects (Peter's knife vs. Clarissa's needle)
notes: The passage demonstrates space-montage by juxtaposing two defensive consciousnesses in the same moment.

passage: What an extraordinary habit that was, Clarissa thought; always playing with a knife. Always making one feel, too, frivolous; empty-minded; a mere silly chatterbox, as he used. But I too, she thought, and, taking up her needle, summoned, like a Queen whose guards have fallen asleep and left her unprotected (she had been quite taken aback by this visit--it had upset her) so that any one can stroll in and have a look at her where she lies with the brambles curving over her, summoned to her help the things she did; the things she liked; her husband; Elizabeth; her self, in short, which Peter hardly knew now, all to come about her and beat off the enemy.
explanation: This passage renders Clarissa’s unspoken thoughts using third-person narration with minimal narratorial intrusion. The diction ('frivolous; empty-minded; a mere silly chatterbox') reflects her inner voice, and the parenthetical aside reveals her emotional vulnerability. Though introduced with 'Clarissa thought,' the content flows associatively from observation to defensive self-summoning, characteristic of indirect interior monologue.
evidence: Use of third-person pronouns with character-specific diction, Parenthetical insertion revealing private emotional state, Associative shift from external observation to internal rallying

passage: (All the same, he is in love, thought Clarissa.)
explanation: A brief but clear instance of indirect interior monologue: the narrator reports Clarissa’s thought using her own phrasing ('All the same, he is in love'), maintaining third-person framing while conveying her immediate, unspoken realization.
evidence: Third-person reporting verb 'thought Clarissa', Colloquial, character-anchored phrasing, Repetition of prior internal conclusion

passage: There they are! he thought. Do what you like with them, Clarissa! There they are! And second by second it seemed to him that the wife of the Major in the Indian Army (his Daisy) and her two small children became more and more lovely as Clarissa looked at them; as if he had set light to a grey pellet on a plate and there had risen up a lovely tree in the brisk sea-salted air of their intimacy (for in some ways no one understood him, felt with him, as Clarissa did)--their exquisite intimacy.
explanation: Peter’s consciousness is rendered through third-person narration that adopts his emotional register and imaginative construction. The metaphor of the 'grey pellet' transforming into a 'lovely tree' enacts his fantasy of how Clarissa perceives his love story, blending memory, projection, and sensory impression.
evidence: Exclamatory internal address ('Do what you like with them, Clarissa!'), Sustained metaphorical imagery reflecting subjective perception, Parenthetical reflection on intimacy unique to Clarissa
notes: The passage merges indirect interior monologue with reverie, as Peter constructs an imagined scene of Clarissa’s empathetic response.

passage: She flattered him; she fooled him, thought Clarissa; shaping the woman, the wife of the Major in the Indian Army, with three strokes of a knife. What a waste! What a folly! All his life long Peter had been fooled like that; first getting sent down from Oxford; next marrying the girl on the boat going out to India; now the wife of a Major in the Indian Army--thank Heaven she had refused to marry him! Still, he was in love; her old friend, her dear Peter, he was in love.
explanation: Clarissa’s judgment of Peter’s romantic pattern unfolds through associative leaps—from present infatuation to past failures—using compressed, emotionally charged syntax. The narrator remains present but yields to Clarissa’s evaluative voice and rhythmic repetitions.
evidence: Reporting clause 'thought Clarissa' embedded mid-flow, Associative chain linking past and present romantic misjudgments, Exclamatory interjections ('What a waste! What a folly!')

passage: For Heaven’s sake, leave your knife alone! she cried to herself in irrepressible irritation; it was his silly unconventionality, his weakness; his lack of the ghost of a notion what any one else was feeling that annoyed her, had always annoyed her; and now at his age, how silly!
explanation: Though framed by the narrator ('she cried to herself'), the passage conveys Clarissa’s immediate, unfiltered irritation in her own idiom. The rhetorical question and emphatic repetitions mimic spontaneous thought, placing this firmly in indirect interior monologue with affective simulation of annoyance.
evidence: Internal exclamation mimicking spoken thought, Repetition for emphasis ('annoyed her, had always annoyed her'), Colloquial judgment ('how silly!')
notes: The affective register is irritation, enacted through abrupt syntax and emphatic diction.

passage: I know all that, Peter thought; I know what I’m up against, he thought, running his finger along the blade of his knife, Clarissa and Dalloway and all the rest of them; but I’ll show Clarissa--and then to his utter surprise, suddenly thrown by those uncontrollable forces thrown through the air, he burst into tears;
explanation: Peter’s defiant internal assertion ('I’ll show Clarissa') collapses abruptly into involuntary emotion, rendered through third-person narration that captures his sudden loss of control. The dash and fragmented syntax simulate the rupture between resolve and feeling.
evidence: First-person internal declarations within third-person frame, Dash signaling abrupt emotional shift, Metaphor of being 'thrown through the air' enacting loss of agency
notes: The passage simulates emotional vulnerability through syntactic disruption and physical description.

passage: If I had married him, this gaiety would have been mine all day! It was all over for her. The sheet was stretched and the bed narrow. She had gone up into the tower alone and left them blackberrying in the sun. The door had shut, and there among the dust of fallen plaster and the litter of birds’ nests how distant the view had looked, and the sounds came thin and chill (once on Leith Hill, she remembered), and Richard, Richard! she cried, as a sleeper in the night starts and stretches a hand in the dark for help.
explanation: Clarissa’s counterfactual fantasy ('If I had married him') triggers a cascade of sensory memories and metaphors of isolation. The narrator renders her consciousness through poetic imagery and abrupt shifts, simulating the melancholic texture of regret.
evidence: Counterfactual conditional initiating reverie, Sensory-rich metaphors of confinement and distance, Parenthetical memory insertion ('once on Leith Hill'), Exclamatory cry ('Richard, Richard!') mimicking dream logic
notes: Blends indirect interior monologue with reverie and affective simulation of melancholy.

passage: Take me with you, Clarissa thought impulsively, as if he were starting directly upon some great voyage; and then, next moment, it was as if the five acts of a play that had been very exciting and moving were now over and she had lived a lifetime in them and had run away, had lived with Peter, and it was now over.
explanation: Clarissa briefly imagines elopement with Peter, constructing a condensed life narrative ('lived with Peter') as theatrical closure. The narrator presents this fantasy using her metaphorical framework ('five acts of a play'), marking it as constructive reverie within indirect interior monologue.
evidence: Impulsive thought introduced with 'Clarissa thought', Theatrical metaphor structuring imagined life, Conditional past perfect ('had lived with Peter')

passage: Remember my party, remember my party, said Peter Walsh as he stepped down the street, speaking to himself rhythmically, in time with the flow of the sound, the direct downright sound of Big Ben striking the half-hour. (The leaden circles dissolved in the air.) Oh these parties, he thought; Clarissa’s parties. Why does she give these parties, he thought.
explanation: Peter’s rhythmic repetition of Clarissa’s phrase blends external sound with internal questioning. The parenthetical '(The leaden circles dissolved in the air)'—a typographic echo of Woolf’s earlier motif—marks a consciousness shift, while his questions reflect associative critique.
evidence: Self-address reported in third person, Parenthetical orthographic marker echoing prior imagery, Rhetorical questions emerging from auditory stimulus
notes: The parenthetical is an orthographic marker signaling poetic intrusion into thought.

passage: Not that he blamed her or this effigy of a man in a tail-coat with a carnation in his buttonhole coming towards him. Only one person in the world could be as he was, in love. And there he was, this fortunate man, himself, reflected in the plate-glass window of a motor-car manufacturer in Victoria Street.
explanation: The narrator describes Peter’s self-perception through his subjective lens—the 'effigy' and 'fortunate man' reflect his romantic self-mythologizing. Though narrated in third person, the imagery belongs to Peter’s consciousness.

notes: Borderline between omniscient description and indirect interior monologue; classified as latter due to sustained alignment with Peter’s self-image.

passage: Clarissa had grown hard, he thought; and a trifle sentimental into the bargain, he suspected, looking at the great motor-cars capable of doing--how many miles on how many gallons? For he had a turn for mechanics; had invented a plough in his district, had ordered wheel-barrows from England, but the coolies wouldn’t use them, all of which Clarissa knew nothing whatever about.
explanation: Peter’s judgment of Clarissa’s character triggers an associative digression into his professional accomplishments, revealing his sense of unappreciated merit. The technical detail and abrupt shift exemplify free association within indirect interior monologue.
evidence: Reporting clauses 'he thought', 'he suspected', Associative leap from sentimentality to mechanical expertise, Resentful emphasis on Clarissa’s ignorance

passage: The way she said “Here is my Elizabeth!”--that annoyed him. Why not “Here’s Elizabeth” simply? It was insincere. And Elizabeth didn’t like it either.
explanation: Peter’s internal critique of Clarissa’s phrasing uses his own colloquial standards ('Why not... simply?') to judge her as 'insincere.' The narrator renders this micro-judgment without commentary, aligning with his perspective.
evidence: Rhetorical question in character’s voice, Moral judgment ('insincere') reflecting personal values, Assumption about Elizabeth’s feelings

passage: Where there is nothing, Peter Walsh said to himself; feeling hollowed out, utterly empty within. Clarissa refused me, he thought. He stood there thinking, Clarissa refused me.
explanation: The repetition of 'Clarissa refused me' and the metaphor of hollowness formally enact Peter’s emotional void. The narrator presents this desolation through stark, recursive phrasing that simulates the state of mind.
evidence: Self-address 'said to himself', Metaphor of emptiness ('hollowed out, utterly empty'), Repetition emphasizing fixation
notes: Clear simulation of emotional emptiness through syntactic and lexical choices.

passage: It is Clarissa herself, he thought, with a deep emotion, and an extraordinarily clear, yet puzzling, recollection of her, as if this bell had come into the room years ago, where they sat at some moment of great intimacy, and had gone from one to the other and had left, like a bee with honey, laden with the moment. But what room? What moment? And why had he been so profoundly happy when the clock was striking?
explanation: Peter associates St. Margaret’s chime with a lost intimate moment, using rich sensory metaphor ('bee with honey'). His unanswered questions mark the associative, elusive quality of memory within indirect interior monologue.
evidence: Reporting clause 'he thought', Synesthetic metaphor linking sound and memory, Unresolved interrogatives reflecting mental drift

passage: Then, as the sound of St. Margaret’s languished, he thought, She has been ill, and the sound expressed languor and suffering. It was her heart, he remembered; and the sudden loudness of the final stroke tolled for death that surprised in the midst of life, Clarissa falling where she stood, in her drawing-room. No! No! he cried. She is not dead!
explanation: Peter’s anxiety transforms the bell’s sound into an omen of Clarissa’s death, rendered through urgent, exclamatory syntax. The narrator captures his panic without mediation, simulating distress through abrupt shifts and imperatives.
evidence: Catastrophic fantasy triggered by auditory cue, Exclamatory denial ('No! No!'), Metaphor of tolling as death knell
notes: Simulates anxiety through fragmented perception and emotional outburst.

passage: I am not old, he cried, and marched up Whitehall, as if there rolled down to him, vigorous, unending, his future.
explanation: Peter’s self-reassurance ('I am not old') is rendered as internal outcry, followed by a metaphor of boundless future. The narrator conveys his emotional rebound through kinetic imagery and declarative force.
evidence: Internal exclamation 'he cried', Metaphor of 'vigorous, unending' future, Physical action mirroring mental shift
notes: Simulates defiant hope through rhythmic, expansive phrasing.

passage: As for caring what they said of him--the Dalloways, the Whitbreads, and their set, he cared not a straw--not a straw (though it was true he would have, some time or other, to see whether Richard couldn’t help him to some job).
explanation: Peter’s assertion of indifference is undercut by a parenthetical admission of dependence, revealing conflicted pride. The narrator presents this self-contradiction through his own rhetorical structure.
evidence: Repetition for emphasis ('not a straw--not a straw'), Parenthetical qualification revealing hidden need, Colloquial dismissal of social judgment

passage: The future lies in the hands of young men like that, he thought. A patter like the patter of leaves in a wood came from behind, and with it a rustling, regular thudding sound, which as it overtook him drummed his thoughts, strict in step, up Whitehall, without his doing.
explanation: Peter’s idealization of youth merges with the marching soldiers’ rhythm, which 'drummed his thoughts.' The narrator renders his consciousness as synchronized with external sound, simulating absorbed contemplation.
evidence: Reporting clause 'he thought', Auditory imagery blending external and internal rhythm, Metaphor of thoughts 'drummed... strict in step'

passage: I can’t keep up with them, Peter Walsh thought, as they marched up Whitehall, and sure enough, on they marched, past him, past every one, in their steady way, as if one will worked legs and arms uniformly, and life, with its varieties, its irreticences, had been laid under a pavement of monuments and wreaths and drugged into a stiff yet staring corpse by discipline.
explanation: Peter’s observation of the soldiers triggers a metaphor of life suppressed by discipline. The narrator channels his critical perspective through dense, funereal imagery that reflects his sense of alienation.
evidence: Internal thought reported mid-observation, Extended metaphor of life as 'stiff yet staring corpse', Contrast between individuality and uniformity

passage: They don’t know the troubles of the flesh yet, he thought, as the marching boys disappeared in the direction of the Strand--all that I’ve been through, he thought, crossing the road, and standing under Gordon’s statue, Gordon whom as a boy he had worshipped; Gordon standing lonely with one leg raised and his arms crossed,--poor Gordon, he thought.
explanation: Peter’s reflection on youthful innocence shifts associatively to his childhood idolization of Gordon, culminating in pity. The narrator renders this chain of thought through his nostalgic, self-comparative lens.
evidence: Repeated 'he thought' anchoring internal progression, Associative link from soldiers to personal history, Empathetic address ('poor Gordon')

passage: And just because nobody yet knew he was in London, except Clarissa, and the earth, after the voyage, still seemed an island to him, the strangeness of standing alone, alive, unknown, at half-past eleven in Trafalgar Square overcame him. What is it? Where am I? And why, after all, does one do it? he thought, the divorce seeming all moonshine.
explanation: Peter’s disorientation manifests in existential questions and the deflation of his divorce plan ('moonshine'). The narrator presents his sudden philosophical vertigo through fragmented inquiry and metaphor.
evidence: Triadic existential questions, Metaphor of 'moonshine' dismissing prior certainty, Emphasis on aloneness and anonymity
notes: Simulates existential doubt through rhetorical questioning and conceptual collapse.

passage: And down his mind went flat as a marsh, and three great emotions bowled over him; understanding; a vast philanthropy; and finally, as if the result of the others, an irrepressible, exquisite delight; as if inside his brain by another hand strings were pulled, shutters moved, and he, having nothing to do with it, yet stood at the opening of endless avenues, down which if he chose he might wander. He had not felt so young for years. He had escaped! was utterly free--as happens in the downfall of habit when the mind, like an unguarded flame, bows and bends and seems about to blow from its holding. I haven’t felt so young for years! thought Peter, escaping (only of course for an hour or so) from being precisely what he was, and feeling like a child who runs out of doors, and sees, as he runs, his old nurse waving at the wrong window.
explanation: Peter’s euphoric release is rendered through cascading metaphors of freedom and renewal. The narrator simulates his exhilaration via flowing syntax, kinetic imagery, and childlike fantasy, all within his subjective frame.
evidence: Metaphors of liberation ('escaped', 'unguarded flame'), Sensory-rich description of mental transformation, Childhood simile expressing regained innocence
notes: Strong simulation of exhilaration through rhythmic, expansive prose.

passage: But she’s extraordinarily attractive, he thought, as, walking across Trafalgar Square in the direction of the Haymarket, came a young woman who, as she passed Gordon’s statue, seemed, Peter Walsh thought (susceptible as he was), to shed veil after veil, until she became the very woman he had always had in mind; young, but stately; merry, but discreet; black, but enchanting.
explanation: Peter projects an idealized feminine archetype onto a stranger, constructing her as 'the very woman he had always had in mind.' The narrator renders this fantasy through lyrical antitheses and metaphorical unveiling.
evidence: Dual reporting clauses emphasizing projection, Antithetical descriptors ('young, but stately; merry, but discreet'), Metaphor of 'shedding veils' enacting idealization
notes: Clear example of constructive reverie within indirect interior monologue.

passage: Straightening himself and stealthily fingering his pocket-knife he started after her to follow this woman, this excitement, which seemed even with its back turned to shed on him a light which connected them, which singled him out, as if the random uproar of the traffic had whispered through hollowed hands his name, not Peter, but his private name which he called himself in his own thoughts. “You,” she said, only “you,” saying it with her white gloves and her shoulders.
explanation: Peter’s pursuit is infused with imagined connection—the woman’s silence becomes speech ('You'), and urban noise transforms into intimate address. The narrator renders his erotic fantasy through synesthetic metaphor.
evidence: Attribution of speech to silent figure, Metaphor of traffic whispering 'private name', Personification of clothing ('saying it with her white gloves')
notes: Constructive fantasy where external reality is reshaped by desire.

passage: But she’s not married; she’s young; quite young, thought Peter, the red carnation he had seen her wear as she came across Trafalgar Square burning again in his eyes and making her lips red. But she waited at the kerbstone. There was a dignity about her. She was not worldly, like Clarissa; not rich, like Clarissa. Was she, he wondered as she moved, respectable?
explanation: Peter’s assessment of the woman’s status and character unfolds through rapid judgments and comparisons to Clarissa. The narrator presents his speculative desire through fragmented observations and rhetorical questioning.
evidence: Reporting clause 'thought Peter', Comparative negations ('not worldly, like Clarissa'), Uncertain interrogation ('Was she... respectable?')

passage: Witty, with a lizard’s flickering tongue, he thought (for one must invent, must allow oneself a little diversion), a cool waiting wit, a darting wit; not noisy. She moved; she crossed; he followed her.
explanation: Peter explicitly acknowledges his fabrication ('one must invent'), yet continues constructing the woman’s personality. The narrator captures his self-aware fantasy through vivid zoological metaphor.
evidence: Parenthetical admission of invention, Metaphor of 'lizard’s flickering tongue', Repetition emphasizing imagined traits
notes: Meta-aware reverie where fantasy is knowingly constructed.

passage: To embarrass her was the last thing he wished. Still if she stopped he would say “Come and have an ice,” he would say, and she would answer, perfectly simply, “Oh yes.”
explanation: Peter scripts an imagined dialogue, projecting simplicity and compliance onto the stranger. The narrator renders this anticipatory fantasy as direct internal rehearsal.
evidence: Conditional scenario ('if she stopped'), Quoted dialogue representing desired outcome, Adverb 'perfectly simply' reflecting wishful thinking
notes: Constructive fantasy of social interaction.

passage: He was an adventurer, reckless, he thought, swift, daring, indeed (landed as he was last night from India) a romantic buccaneer, careless of all these damned proprieties, yellow dressing-gowns, pipes, fishing-rods, in the shop windows; and respectability and evening parties and spruce old men wearing white slips beneath their waistcoats. He was a buccaneer.
explanation: Peter inflates his identity into a 'romantic buccaneer,' contrasting his imagined freedom against symbols of bourgeois conformity. The narrator renders this self-dramatization through emphatic repetition and catalogued details.
evidence: Self-attribution 'he thought', Repetition for self-persuasion ('He was a buccaneer'), Catalogue of rejected social signifiers

passage: Well, I’ve had my fun; I’ve had it, he thought, looking up at the swinging baskets of pale geraniums. And it was smashed to atoms--his fun, for it was half made up, as he knew very well; invented, this escapade with the girl; made up, as one makes up the better part of life, he thought--making oneself up; making her up; creating an exquisite amusement, and something more.
explanation: Peter reflects on the constructed nature of his fantasy, acknowledging its artifice while affirming its value. The narrator renders his meta-cognitive insight through recursive phrasing and philosophical generalization.
evidence: Self-quotation 'I’ve had my fun', Admission of invention ('half made up'), Generalization about life as construction
notes: Simulates bittersweet clarity through reflective, almost aphoristic phrasing.

passage: Well, I’ve had my fun; I’ve had it, he thought, looking up at the swinging baskets of pale geraniums. And it was smashed to atoms--his fun, for it was half made up, as he knew very well; invented, this escapade with the girl; made up, as one makes up the better part of life, he thought--making oneself up; making her up; creating an exquisite amusement, and something more.

passage: His boots on the pavement struck out “no matter”; for it was early, still very early. It was a splendid morning too. Like the pulse of a perfect heart, life struck straight through the streets. There was no fumbling--no hesitation.

evidence: Narrator-controlled simile ('Like the pulse of a perfect heart'), Descriptive syntax belonging to narrator, not character

passage: Coming as he did from a respectable Anglo-Indian family which for at least three generations had administered the affairs of a continent (it’s strange, he thought, what a sentiment I have about that, disliking India, and empire, and army as he did), there were moments when civilisation, even of this sort, seemed dear to him as a personal possession; moments of pride in England; in butlers; chow dogs; girls in their security. explanation: The passage blends third-person narration with Peter’s self-reflective voice. evidence: Parenthetical thought insertion reflecting character’s idiom, Reporting verb used minimally at end, Content and diction ('Ridiculous enough') belong to Peter

passage: And the doctors and men of business and capable women all going about their business, punctual, alert, robust, seemed to him wholly admirable, good fellows, to whom one would entrust one’s life, companions in the art of living, who would see one through.

passage: Odd, he thought, how the thought of childhood keeps coming back to me--the result of seeing Clarissa, perhaps; for women live much more in the past than we do, he thought. They attach themselves to places; and their fathers--a woman’s always proud of her father.

passage: Bourton was a nice place, a very nice place, but I could never get on with the old man, he thought.

evidence: First-person embedded in third-person narration, Reporting verb confirms character POV

passage: She’s a queer-looking girl, he thought, suddenly remembering Elizabeth as she came into the room and stood by her mother. passage: I shall try and get a word alone with Elizabeth to-night, he thought--then began to wobble into hour-glass shapes and taper away; odd shapes they take, he thought.

passage: Suddenly he closed his eyes, raised his hand with an effort, and threw away the heavy end of his cigar. explanation: The narrator describes Peter’s descent into sleep using poetic, externalized imagery ('great brush,' 'plumes and feathers'). The consciousness is reported, not rendered directly, with the narrator fully in control of the metaphorical language.
evidence: Narrator-controlled metaphors, Descriptive syntax not mimicking thought process

passage: By conviction an atheist perhaps, he is taken by surprise with moments of extraordinary exaltation. Nothing exists outside us except a state of mind, he thinks; a desire for solace, for relief, for something outside these miserable pigmies, these feeble, these ugly, these craven men and women. But if he can conceive of her, then in some sort she exists, he thinks, and advancing down the path with his eyes upon sky and branches he rapidly endows them with womanhood; sees with amazement how grave they become; how majestically, as the breeze stirs them, they dispense with a dark flutter of the leaves charity, comprehension, absolution, and then, flinging themselves suddenly aloft, confound the piety of their aspect with a wild carouse.
explanation: The narrator describes the inner experience of a generalized 'solitary traveller,' blending philosophical reflection with constructed fantasy. evidence: Generalized subject ('he') not tied to named character, Narrator constructs extended metaphorical sequence, Philosophical assertions framed by narrator
notes: Though the content involves reverie/fantasy, the technique is omniscient description because the narrator fully mediates the consciousness.

passage: So, he thinks, may I never go back to the lamplight; to the sitting-room; never finish my book; never knock out my pipe; never ring for Mrs. Turner to clear away; rather let me walk straight on to this great figure, who will, with a toss of her head, mount me on her streamers and let me blow to nothingness with the rest.
explanation: The narrator reports the traveller’s imagined rejection of domesticity and embrace of dissolution. Though the content is first-person desire ('may I never'), it is embedded in third-person narration with full narratorial control, making it omniscient description of a fantasy state.

passage:

passage: It became clearer; the scene, the room, the past he had been dreaming of. It was at Bourton that summer, early in the ’nineties, when he was so passionately in love with Clarissa. evidence: Conventional narrative syntax, Narrator sets scene ('bathed in yellow light'), No rendering of memory retrieval process

passage: He could see Clarissa now, turning bright pink; somehow contracting; and saying, “Oh, I shall never be able to speak to her again!” Whereupon the whole party sitting round the tea-table seemed to wobble. evidence: Character-specific evaluative language, Repetition of key phrase as internal refrain, Associative movement from observation to judgment

passage: Every one wobbled; every one seemed to bow, as she spoke, and then to stand up different. He could see Sally Seton, like a child who has been in mischief, leaning forward, rather flushed, wanting to talk, but afraid, and Clarissa did frighten people.

evidence: Narrator describes visual impressions ('like a child'), No direct rendering of Peter’s thought process

passage: (She was Clarissa’s greatest friend, always about the place, totally unlike her, an attractive creature, handsome, dark, with the reputation in those days of great daring and he used to give her cigars, which she smoked in her bedroom. evidence: Narrator labels emotion ('extremely depressed'), Summary of cyclical behavior in narrator’s voice

passage: (The place was quite a humble one; the Parrys were never very well off; but there were always grooms and stable-boys about--Clarissa loved riding--and an old coachman--what was his name?--an old nurse, old Moody, old Goody, some such name they called her, whom one was taken to visit in a little room with lots of photographs, lots of bird-cages.)
explanation: This parenthetical offers setting details in the narrator’s expository voice, including a rhetorical question ('what was his name?') that belongs to the narrator, not Peter’s consciousness.

passage: It was an awful evening! He grew more and more gloomy, not about that only; about everything. And he couldn’t see her; couldn’t explain to her; couldn’t have it out. passage: All through dinner he tried to hear what they were saying. Afterwards he could remember standing by old Miss Parry’s chair in the drawing-room.
