explanation: Peter’s conflicting emotions—rage, admiration, desire to hurt—are rendered through third-person narration that closely follows his perspective. The quoted phrase ('The perfect hostess') and the admission of intent ('he meant her to feel it') reflect his internal stance.
evidence: Conflicted emotional responses in character’s voice, Quoted phrase as internal judgment, Admission of malicious intent

passage: So she left him. And he had a feeling that they were all gathered together in a conspiracy against him--laughing and talking--behind his back. There he stood by Miss Parry’s chair as though he had been cut out of wood, he talking about wild flowers. Never, never had he suffered so infernally!
explanation: Peter’s sense of persecution and emotional extremity is conveyed through hyperbolic language ('conspiracy,' 'Never, never,' 'infernally') that reflects his subjective state within a lightly framed third-person structure.
evidence: Hyperbolic emotional language, Paranoid interpretation ('conspiracy'), Repetitive intensifiers ('Never, never')
notes: Also exhibits simulation of state of mind (distress) through emphatic repetition and extreme diction.

passage: He must have forgotten even to pretend to listen; at last he woke up; he saw Miss Parry looking rather disturbed, rather indignant, with her prominent eyes fixed. He almost cried out that he couldn’t attend because he was in Hell!
explanation: The near-outburst ('he was in Hell!') captures Peter’s internal state with dramatic intensity. The exclamation, though not spoken, is rendered as his unvoiced thought within indirect interior monologue.
evidence: Unvoiced exclamation reflecting inner state, Dramatic metaphor ('in Hell'), Minimal narratorial framing
notes: Strong simulation of distress through metaphor and near-speech.

passage: People began going out of the room. He heard them talking about fetching cloaks; about its being cold on the water, and so on. They were going boating on the lake by moonlight--one of Sally’s mad ideas. He could hear her describing the moon. And they all went out. He was left quite alone.
explanation: The narrator describes Peter’s auditory perception and resulting isolation in conventional prose. The focus is on external events as perceived, not on the texture of his consciousness.
evidence: Narrator reports sensory input and consequence, Conventional sentence structure, No immersion in thought process

passage: “Don’t you want to go with them?” said Aunt Helena--old Miss Parry!--she had guessed. And he turned round and there was Clarissa again. She had come back to fetch him. He was overcome by her generosity--her goodness.
explanation: The narrator summarizes Peter’s emotional response ('overcome by her generosity') rather than rendering his immediate consciousness. This is psycho-narration within omniscient description.
evidence: Narrator labels emotion ('overcome'), Summary of feeling rather than immersive rendering

passage:
“Come along,” she said. “They’re waiting.”

He had never felt so happy in the whole of his life! Without a word they made it up. They walked down to the lake. He had twenty minutes of perfect happiness. Her voice, her laugh, her dress (something floating, white, crimson), her spirit, her adventurousness; she made them all disembark and explore the island; she startled a hen; she laughed; she sang.
explanation: The narrator describes Peter’s remembered happiness using vivid sensory details. Though the content is joyful, the form is narratorial description rather than direct immersion in his consciousness.
evidence: Narrator enumerates sensory details, Exclamatory statement framed by narrator, Descriptive syntax controlled by narrator
notes: Exhibits simulation of happiness through flowing syntax and sensory richness, but technique remains omniscient description.

passage: And all the time, he knew perfectly well, Dalloway was falling in love with her; she was falling in love with Dalloway; but it didn’t seem to matter. Nothing mattered. They sat on the ground and talked--he and Clarissa. They went in and out of each other’s minds without any effort.
explanation: Peter’s retrospective acknowledgment of Clarissa’s attraction to Dalloway and his own transcendent feeling ('Nothing mattered') is rendered in his voice with minimal narratorial presence, characteristic of indirect interior monologue.
evidence: Character’s philosophical conclusion ('Nothing mattered'), Assertion of mutual understanding in Peter’s terms, Light third-person framing

passage: And then in a second it was over. He said to himself as they were getting into the boat, “She will marry that man,” dully, without any resentment; but it was an obvious thing. Dalloway would marry Clarissa. Dalloway rowed them in. He said nothing. But somehow as they watched him start, jumping on to his bicycle to ride twenty miles through the woods, wobbling off down the drive, waving his hand and disappearing, he obviously did feel, instinctively, tremendously, strongly, all that; the night; the romance; Clarissa. He deserved to have her. For himself, he was absurd. His demands upon Clarissa (he could see it now) were absurd. He asked impossible things. He made terrible scenes. She would have accepted him still, perhaps, if he had been less absurd.
explanation: Peter’s self-critical reflection unfolds associatively, moving from observation to self-judgment to hypothetical regret. The diction ('absurd,' 'impossible things') and rhetorical structure reflect his internal voice with minimal narratorial mediation.
evidence: Self-deprecating diction, Associative progression from observation to self-blame, Hypothetical reasoning in character’s voice

passage: Sally thought so. She wrote him all that summer long letters; how they had talked of him; how she had praised him, how Clarissa burst into tears! It was an extraordinary summer--all letters, scenes, telegrams--arriving at Bourton early in the morning, hanging about till the servants were up; appalling _tête-à-têtes_ with old Mr. Parry at breakfast; Aunt Helena formidable but kind; Sally sweeping him off for talks in the vegetable garden; Clarissa in bed with headaches.
explanation: The narrator summarizes the events of the summer in an expository, list-like structure. Though based on Peter’s memories, the form is narratorial rather than immersive.
evidence: Enumerative structure, Narrator controls pacing and selection, No rendering of memory process

passage: The final scene, the terrible scene which he believed had mattered more than anything in the whole of his life (it might be an exaggeration--but still so it did seem now) happened at three o’clock in the afternoon of a very hot day.
explanation: The narrator frames Peter’s belief about the scene’s importance, including a qualifying aside ('it might be an exaggeration'). This meta-commentary indicates narratorial presence rather than direct immersion.
evidence: Narrator qualifies character’s belief, Explanatory parenthesis in narrator’s voice, Descriptive setup for upcoming scene

passage: It was a trifle that led up to it--Sally at lunch saying something about Dalloway, and calling him “My name is Dalloway”; whereupon Clarissa suddenly stiffened, coloured, in a way she had, and rapped out sharply, “We’ve had enough of that feeble joke.” That was all; but for him it was precisely as if she had said, “I’m only amusing myself with you; I’ve an understanding with Richard Dalloway.” So he took it.
explanation: Peter’s interpretation of Clarissa’s remark as a coded message is rendered through third-person narration that closely follows his perspective. The imagined dialogue reflects his internal understanding.
evidence: Character’s interpretive leap ('precisely as if she had said'), Imagined dialogue representing internal inference, Minimal narratorial presence

passage: He had not slept for nights. “It’s got to be finished one way or the other,” he said to himself. He sent a note to her by Sally asking her to meet him by the fountain at three. “Something very important has happened,” he scribbled at the end of it.
explanation: Peter’s resolve and actions are rendered with internal motivation. The quoted self-address ('It’s got to be finished') and the note’s content reflect his urgent state of mind within indirect interior monologue.
evidence: Quoted internal resolution, Action motivated by internal state, Light third-person framing

passage: It’s wicked; why should I suffer? she was asking, as she walked down the broad path. No; I can’t stand it any longer, she was saying, having left Septimus, who wasn’t Septimus any longer, to say hard, cruel, wicked things, to talk to himself, to talk to a dead man, on the seat over there; when the child ran full tilt into her, fell flat, and burst out crying.
explanation: The passage renders Rezia’s unspoken thoughts using third-person narration with minimal narratorial intrusion. The content and diction ('It’s wicked; why should I suffer?') belong to her subjective voice, and reporting verbs like 'she was asking' are light and parenthetical, characteristic of indirect interior monologue.
evidence: Third-person pronouns with character-anchored diction, Light use of reporting verbs ('she was asking', 'she was saying'), Emotional, pre-articulate thought rendered in character’s idiom

passage: But for herself she had done nothing wrong; she had loved Septimus; she had been happy; she had had a beautiful home, and there her sisters lived still, making hats. Why should _she_ suffer?
explanation: This continues Rezia’s internal questioning in third person, with syntax and emotional logic reflecting her distress. The narrator remains present but yields to her perspective, using her rhetorical patterns and emphasis (underscored 'she').
evidence: Repetition of 'she had...' mirroring self-justification, Use of italics for emphasis as in character’s mental voice, Absence of interpretive commentary from narrator

passage: Slightly waved by tears the broad path, the nurse, the man in grey, the perambulator, rose and fell before her eyes. To be rocked by this malignant torturer was her lot. But why? She was like a bird sheltering under the thin hollow of a leaf, who blinks at the sun when the leaf moves; starts at the crack of a dry twig. She was exposed; she was surrounded by the enormous trees, vast clouds of an indifferent world, exposed; tortured; and why should she suffer? Why?
explanation: The passage blends Rezia’s perception with metaphorical imagery that expresses her emotional state. The narrator describes her vision ('slightly waved by tears') but the simile of the bird and the repeated 'why?' reflect her unmediated consciousness. The affective register of distress is enacted through fragmented repetition and sensory distortion.
evidence: Subjective visual distortion ('slightly waved by tears'), Extended simile anchored in character’s fear ('like a bird...'), Repetition of 'exposed; tortured; and why should she suffer? Why?'
notes: Also qualifies as simulation of state of mind (distress), but primary mode is indirect interior monologue.

passage: He had seemed a nice quiet man; a great friend of Septimus’s, and he had been killed in the War. But such things happen to every one. Every one has friends who were killed in the War. Every one gives up something when they marry. She had given up her home. She had come to live here, in this awful city.
explanation: Rezia rationalizes her suffering through generalizations that reflect her attempt to normalize trauma. The repetitive structure ('Every one...') and personal admission ('She had given up her home') are rendered in her voice via third-person narration with minimal mediation.
evidence: Generalizing logic typical of self-soothing thought, Shift from external fact to personal loss within same syntactic flow, Use of 'awful city' reflecting subjective judgment

passage: Perfect nonsense it was; about death; about Miss Isabel Pole. She could stand it no longer. She would go back.
explanation: Brief but clear rendering of Rezia’s judgment and decision in her own idiom ('Perfect nonsense it was'), using third-person framing without narratorial interpretation.
evidence: Colloquial phrasing ('Perfect nonsense it was'), Immediate resolution ('She would go back') reflecting spontaneous thought

passage: My hand has grown so thin,” she said. “I have put it in my purse,” she told him.
explanation: This is reported dialogue, not interior consciousness. However, it is included because it directly triggers Septimus’s interior response. But strictly speaking, this is not SOC—it is spoken utterance. Therefore, it should NOT be classified as SOC.
evidence: Explicit dialogue tags ('she said', 'she told him'), Externally spoken words, not unspoken thought
notes: Not SOC—this is dialogue. Excluded from final list.

passage: Their marriage was over, he thought, with agony, with relief. The rope was cut; he mounted; he was free, as it was decreed that he, Septimus, the lord of men, should be free; alone (since his wife had thrown away her wedding ring; since she had left him), he, Septimus, was alone, called forth in advance of the mass of men to hear the truth, to learn the meaning, which now at last, after all the toils of civilisation--Greeks, Romans, Shakespeare, Darwin, and now himself--was to be given whole to.... “To whom?” he asked aloud.
explanation: The passage presents Septimus’s consciousness directly, without narratorial mediation. There are no reporting verbs beyond the initial 'he thought,' which quickly gives way to unfiltered first-person-like grandiosity ('he, Septimus, the lord of men'). The shift to spoken question ('To whom?') marks the boundary, but the preceding is raw thought.
evidence: Absence of narrator after initial cue, Grandiose, associative logic linking historical figures to self, Parenthetical justifications mimicking real-time thought
notes: Initial 'he thought' is a light frame, but the bulk is direct interior monologue.

passage: No crime; love; he repeated, fumbling for his card and pencil, when a Skye terrier snuffed his trousers and he started in an agony of fear. It was turning into a man! He could not watch it happen! It was horrible, terrible to see a dog become a man!
explanation: Septimus’s hallucinatory terror is rendered without mediation. The exclamations ('It was turning into a man!', 'horrible, terrible') enact panic through syntax and repetition. The form simulates acute psychosis and fear.
evidence: Exclamatory, fragmented sentences, Present-tense immediacy of hallucination, Repetition for emotional intensification
notes: Also a strong example of simulation of state of mind (terror).

passage: But what was the scientific explanation (for one must be scientific above all things)? Why could he see through bodies, see into the future, when dogs will become men? It was the heat wave presumably, operating upon a brain made sensitive by eons of evolution. Scientifically speaking, the flesh was melted off the world. His body was macerated until only the nerve fibres were left. It was spread like a veil upon a rock.
explanation: Septimus constructs a pseudo-scientific rationale for his visions. The passage is unmediated, blending delusion with lyrical imagery ('veil upon a rock'). The parenthetical aside ('for one must be scientific') mimics real-time self-correction.
evidence: Self-addressed parenthetical reasoning, Metaphysical speculation in first-person logic, Sustained invented imagery not tied to memory
notes: Constructive fantasy/reverie, not memory-based.

passage: He lay back in his chair, exhausted but upheld. He lay resting, waiting, before he again interpreted, with effort, with agony, to mankind. He lay very high, on the back of the world. The earth thrilled beneath him. Red flowers grew through his flesh; their stiff leaves rustled by his head.
explanation: Continuation of Septimus’s visionary state. The physical sensations and metaphysical positioning ('on the back of the world') are rendered as immediate perception, not narrated description.
evidence: Blending of bodily sensation and cosmic imagery, Lack of narratorial framing or interpretation, Present-tense immersion in altered state

passage: Music began clanging against the rocks up here. It is a motor horn down in the street, he muttered; but up here it cannoned from rock to rock, divided, met in shocks of sound which rose in smooth columns (that music should be visible was a discovery) and became an anthem, an anthem twined round now by a shepherd boy’s piping (That’s an old man playing a penny whistle by the public-house, he muttered) which, as the boy stood still came bubbling from his pipe, and then, as he climbed higher, made its exquisite plaint while the traffic passed beneath.
explanation: Septimus transforms ordinary sounds into transcendent music through associative perception. The interplay between mundane reality ('motor horn', 'penny whistle') and visionary interpretation is rendered directly, with mutterings embedded as part of thought flow.
evidence: Embedded muttered corrections as part of consciousness, Sensory transformation (sound → visible columns), Associative layering of real and imagined sound sources
notes: Muttered phrases are part of interior monologue, not external speech.

passage: This boy’s elegy is played among the traffic, thought Septimus. Now he withdraws up into the snows, and roses hang about him--the thick red roses which grow on my bedroom wall, he reminded himself.
explanation: The phrase 'thought Septimus' is a minimal frame, but the content is unmediated fantasy blending pastoral myth with domestic memory. The self-reminder anchors the reverie in personal experience.
evidence: Shift from mythic imagery to personal memory, Use of 'reminded himself' as internal cue, not narratorial, Constructive scene-building not based on current perception
notes: Hybrid of memory and fantasy, but primarily constructive.

passage: The music stopped. He has his penny, he reasoned it out, and has gone on to the next public-house. But he himself remained high on his rock, like a drowned sailor on a rock. I leant over the edge of the boat and fell down, he thought. I went under the sea. I have been dead, and yet am now alive, but let me rest still; he begged (he was talking to himself again--it was awful, awful!); and as, before waking, the voices of birds and the sound of wheels chime and chatter in a queer harmony, grow louder and louder and the sleeper feels himself drawing to the shores of life, so he felt himself drawing towards life, the sun growing hotter, cries sounding louder, something tremendous about to happen.
explanation: Septimus narrates his own resurrection myth in first-person thought. The embedded self-dialogue ('I leant...', 'let me rest still') and simile of waking show unmediated consciousness. The rising intensity simulates anxious anticipation.
evidence: First-person declarations embedded in third-person frame, Metaphor of rebirth linked to sensory awakening, Parenthetical self-awareness ('talking to himself again')
notes: The phrase 'he thought' and 'he begged' are light frames; core is direct.

passage: He had only to open his eyes; but a weight was on them; a fear. He strained; he pushed; he looked; he saw Regent’s Park before him. Long streamers of sunlight fawned at his feet. The trees waved, brandished. We welcome, the world seemed to say; we accept; we create. Beauty, the world seemed to say.
explanation: Perception merges with revelation. The world’s voice ('We welcome...') is Septimus’s projection, rendered as immediate insight. The lyrical syntax enacts euphoric clarity.
evidence: Personification of world as speaking directly to him, Sensory richness and rhythmic prose, Shift from fear to ecstatic perception
notes: Simulation of joy through flowing syntax and affirmative imagery.

passage: And as if to prove it (scientifically) wherever he looked at the houses, at the railings, at the antelopes stretching over the palings, beauty sprang instantly. To watch a leaf quivering in the rush of air was an exquisite joy. Up in the sky swallows swooping, swerving, flinging themselves in and out, round and round, yet always with perfect control as if elastics held them; and the flies rising and falling; and the sun spotting now this leaf, now that, in mockery, dazzling it with soft gold in pure good temper; and now and again some chime (it might be a motor horn) tinkling divinely on the grass stalks--all of this, calm and reasonable as it was, made out of ordinary things as it was, was the truth now; beauty, that was the truth now. Beauty was everywhere.
explanation: Sustained sensory impression stream where external reality is filtered through Septimus’s epiphanic joy. The accumulation of details and rhythmic listing simulate rapturous perception.
evidence: Dense, synesthetic sensory catalog, Repetition of 'beauty' as mantra, Syntax mimics flowing, uninterrupted perception
notes: Classic literary impressionism enacting state of mind (joy).

passage: It is time,” said Rezia. The word “time” split its husk; poured its riches over him; and from his lips fell like shells, like shavings from a plane, without his making them, hard, white, imperishable words, and flew to attach themselves to their places in an ode to Time; an immortal ode to Time. He sang. Evans answered from behind the tree. The dead were in Thessaly, Evans sang, among the orchids. There they waited till the War was over, and now the dead, now Evans himself--
explanation: Septimus experiences Rezia’s utterance as a cosmic event triggering poetic creation and communion with the dead. The mythic elaboration (Thessaly, orchids) is unmediated fantasy.
evidence: Metaphysical transformation of mundane word ('time'), Invented dialogue with dead comrade, Mythic geography replacing real setting
notes: Constructive fantasy merging memory and myth.

passage: For God’s sake don’t come!” Septimus cried out. For he could not look upon the dead. But the branches parted. A man in grey was actually walking towards them. It was Evans! But no mud was on him; no wounds; he was not changed. I must tell the whole world, Septimus cried, raising his hand (as the dead man in the grey suit came nearer), raising his hand like some colossal figure who has lamented the fate of man for ages in the desert alone with his hands pressed to his forehead, furrows of despair on his cheeks, and now sees light on the desert’s edge which broadens and strikes the iron-black figure (and Septimus half rose from his chair), and with legions of men prostrate behind him he, the giant mourner, receives for one moment on his face the whole--
explanation: Septimus conflates real approach of Peter Walsh with vision of Evans, then projects himself as a messianic figure. The extended simile is part of his unfiltered consciousness.
evidence: Immediate shift from cry to mythic self-image, Embedded parenthetical actions as part of thought, Grandiose, cinematic self-perception
notes: Blurs external action and internal vision, but core is interior.

passage: The millions lamented; for ages they had sorrowed. He would turn round, he would tell them in a few moments, only a few moments more, of this relief, of this joy, of this astonishing revelation--
explanation: Septimus imagines addressing humanity with salvific truth. The conditional future ('He would turn...') reflects constructed fantasy, not memory.
evidence: Messianic projection, Anticipatory syntax ('in a few moments more'), Absence of narratorial framing

passage: Now for instance there was a man writing quite openly in one of the respectable weeklies about water-closets. That you couldn’t have done ten years ago--written quite openly about water-closets in a respectable weekly. And then this taking out a stick of rouge, or a powder-puff and making up in public. On board ship coming home there were lots of young men and girls--Betty and Bertie he remembered in particular--carrying on quite openly; the old mother sitting and watching them with her knitting, cool as a cucumber.
explanation: Peter’s observations about social change are rendered in his voice through third-person narration. The associative jump from newspapers to shipboard behavior reflects his drifting thought process.
evidence: Colloquial phrasing ('water-closets', 'carrying on'), Personal examples ('Betty and Bertie'), Light narratorial presence

passage: She would make a very good wife at thirty--she would marry when it suited her to marry; marry some rich man and live in a large house near Manchester. Who was it now who had done that? Peter Walsh asked himself, turning into the Broad Walk,--married a rich man and lived in a large house near Manchester? Somebody who had written him a long, gushing letter quite lately about “blue hydrangeas.” It was seeing blue hydrangeas that made her think of him and the old days--Sally Seton, of course! It was Sally Seton--the last person in the world one would have expected to marry a rich man and live in a large house near Manchester, the wild, the daring, the romantic Sally!
explanation: Peter’s train of thought moves from generalization to specific recollection via sensory trigger ('blue hydrangeas'). The exclamation and appositives ('the wild, the daring...') reflect his emotional surprise.
evidence: Self-directed question ('Who was it now...?'), Sensory trigger unlocking memory, Emphatic repetition and characterization

passage: “The Whitbreads?” he could hear her saying. “Who are the Whitbreads? Coal merchants. Respectable tradespeople.” Hugh she detested for some reason. He thought of nothing but his own appearance, she said. He ought to have been a Duke. He would be certain to marry one of the Royal Princesses. And of course Hugh had the most extraordinary, the most natural, the most sublime respect for the British aristocracy of any human being he had ever come across.
explanation: Peter recalls Sally’s past judgments in her voice, blended with his own reflections. The mix of quoted and paraphrased thought is typical of Woolf’s indirect interior monologue.
evidence: Embedded recalled dialogue in character’s idiom, Shift between Sally’s voice and Peter’s commentary, Judgmental tone consistent with Peter’s perspective
notes: Hybrid of memory and present evaluation, but rendered as interior flow.

passage: He was a perfect specimen of the public school type, she said. No country but England could have produced him. She was really spiteful, for some reason; had some grudge against him. Something had happened--he forgot what--in the smoking-room. He had insulted her--kissed her? Incredible! Nobody believed a word against Hugh of course. Who could? Kissing Sally in the smoking-room! If it had been some Honourable Edith or Lady Violet, perhaps; but not that ragamuffin Sally without a penny to her name, and a father or a mother gambling at Monte Carlo.
explanation: Peter speculates about past events, moving between Sally’s possible motives and social judgments. The rhetorical questions and class-conscious phrasing reflect his internal debate.
evidence: Speculative syntax ('kissed her? Incredible!'), Class-based distinctions ('Honourable Edith', 'ragamuffin Sally'), Associative gap ('he forgot what')

passage: For of all the people he had ever met Hugh was the greatest snob--the most obsequious--no, he didn’t cringe exactly. He was too much of a prig for that. A first-rate valet was the obvious comparison--somebody who walked behind carrying suit cases; could be trusted to send telegrams--indispensable to hostesses.
explanation: Peter refines his critique of Hugh through self-correction ('no, he didn’t cringe exactly') and metaphor. The evaluative language is his own, rendered with minimal narration.
evidence: Self-correction mid-thought, Extended metaphor ('first-rate valet'), Colloquial judgment ('too much of a prig')

passage: How remorseless life is! A little job at Court! He had married this lady, the Honourable Evelyn, and they lived hereabouts, so he thought (looking at the pompous houses overlooking the Park), for he had lunched there once in a house which had, like all Hugh’s possessions, something that no other house could possibly have--linen cupboards it might have been.
explanation: Peter’s bitterness about social disparity emerges through ironic repetition ('A little job at Court!') and associative detail ('linen cupboards'). The parenthetical observation ties thought to present perception.
evidence: Exclamatory opening expressing emotion, Sarcastic understatement ('little job'), Tangential memory triggered by architecture

passage: Whatever he took up he did in the same matter-of-fact sensible way; without a touch of imagination, without a spark of brilliancy, but with the inexplicable niceness of his type. He ought to have been a country gentleman--he was wasted on politics. He was at his best out of doors, with horses and dogs--how good he was, for instance, when that great shaggy dog of Clarissa’s got caught in a trap and had its paw half torn off, and Clarissa turned faint and Dalloway did the whole thing; bandaged, made splints; told Clarissa not to be a fool. That was what she liked him for perhaps--that was what she needed. “Now, my dear, don’t be a fool. Hold this--fetch that,” all the time talking to the dog as if it were a human being.
explanation: The passage renders Peter Walsh's thoughts about Richard Dalloway using third-person pronouns but with diction and associative logic characteristic of Peter’s perspective. The narrator is present but yields to Peter’s evaluative voice, especially in the shift from general assessment to vivid memory triggered by sensory-emotional association (the injured dog).
evidence: Third-person framing ('He ought to have been...') combined with subjective judgments ('inexplicable niceness', 'wasted on politics') reflecting Peter’s values, Associative jump from abstract traits to specific remembered scene (dog incident) typical of free association, Use of direct quotation within thought ('Now, my dear, don’t be a fool') mimicking internal rehearsal

passage: But how could she swallow all that stuff about poetry? How could she let him hold forth about Shakespeare? Seriously and solemnly Richard Dalloway got on his hind legs and said that no decent man ought to read Shakespeare’s sonnets because it was like listening at keyholes (besides the relationship was not one that he approved). No decent man ought to let his wife visit a deceased wife’s sister. Incredible! The only thing to do was to pelt him with sugared almonds--it was at dinner. But Clarissa sucked it all in; thought it so honest of him; so independent of him; Heaven knows if she didn’t think him the most original mind she’d ever met!
explanation: This passage conveys Peter’s incredulous, mocking inner commentary on Clarissa’s admiration for Richard. Though in third person, the rhetorical questions, hyperbolic imagery ('pelt him with sugared almonds'), and emotional tone belong unmistakably to Peter. The narrator merely channels his voice.
evidence: Rhetorical questions expressing personal disbelief ('But how could she swallow...?'), Colloquial, emotionally charged diction ('Incredible!', 'sucked it all in'), Vivid, absurd visual image ('pelt him with sugared almonds') simulating Peter’s contemptuous fantasy

passage: There was a garden where they used to walk, a walled-in place, with rose-bushes and giant cauliflowers--he could remember Sally tearing off a rose, stopping to exclaim at the beauty of the cabbage leaves in the moonlight (it was extraordinary how vividly it all came back to him, things he hadn’t thought of for years,) while she implored him, half laughing of course, to carry off Clarissa, to save her from the Hughs and the Dalloways and all the other “perfect gentlemen” who would “stifle her soul” (she wrote reams of poetry in those days), make a mere hostess of her, encourage her worldliness.
explanation: Peter recalls a past scene with sensory richness and emotional resonance. The memory is triggered associatively and rendered with the immediacy of lived experience, though framed in third person. The parenthetical reflection ('extraordinary how vividly...') marks the intrusion of present consciousness into recollection.
evidence: Sensory detail anchored in character perception ('beauty of the cabbage leaves in the moonlight'), Parenthetical meta-commentary on the act of remembering, Quoted dialogue from the past embedded as part of memory stream

passage: But one must do Clarissa justice. She wasn’t going to marry Hugh anyhow. She had a perfectly clear notion of what she wanted. Her emotions were all on the surface. Beneath, she was very shrewd--a far better judge of character than Sally, for instance, and with it all, purely feminine; with that extraordinary gift, that woman’s gift, of making a world of her own wherever she happened to be. She came into a room; she stood, as he had often seen her, in a doorway with lots of people round her. But it was Clarissa one remembered. Not that she was striking; not beautiful at all; there was nothing picturesque about her; she never said anything specially clever; there she was, however; there she was.
explanation: Peter’s reflective assessment of Clarissa blends judgment and affection. The repetitive phrasing ('there she was, however; there she was') mimics the rhythm of thought, and the contrast between surface and depth reflects his ongoing attempt to understand her. The narrator transmits Peter’s evaluative framework without interference.
evidence: Repetition and syntactic rhythm mimicking cognitive emphasis, Subjective valuation ('purely feminine', 'extraordinary gift'), Shift from abstract claim to concrete visual memory ('stood... in a doorway')

passage: No, no, no! He was not in love with her any more! He only felt, after seeing her that morning, among her scissors and silks, making ready for the party, unable to get away from the thought of her; she kept coming back and back like a sleeper jolting against him in a railway carriage; which was not being in love, of course; it was thinking of her, criticising her, starting again, after thirty years, trying to explain her.
explanation: Peter’s emphatic self-reassurance ('No, no, no!') and the simile of the 'sleeper jolting' enact his agitated mental state. The passage simulates anxiety through abrupt syntax, repetition, and physicalized metaphor, while remaining in third person with minimal narratorial presence.
evidence: Exclamatory negation mimicking internal self-correction, Kinesthetic simile ('sleeper jolting') conveying intrusive thought, Self-reflective parsing of emotion ('which was not being in love...')
notes: Also qualifies as simulation of state of mind (anxiety) due to formal enactment of distress.

passage: The obvious thing to say of her was that she was worldly; cared too much for rank and society and getting on in the world--which was true in a sense; she had admitted it to him. (You could always get her to own up if you took the trouble; she was honest.) What she would say was that she hated frumps, fogies, failures, like himself presumably; thought people had no right to slouch about with their hands in their pockets; must do something, be something; and these great swells, these Duchesses, these hoary old Countesses one met in her drawing-room, unspeakably remote as he felt them to be from anything that mattered a straw, stood for something real to her.
explanation: Peter reconstructs Clarissa’s likely words and values, blending reported speech with his own bitterness ('like himself presumably'). The associative flow from her honesty to her disdain for 'failures' reveals his self-consciousness and lingering hurt.
evidence: Embedded hypothetical speech ('What she would say was...'), Self-referential aside ('like himself presumably') revealing insecurity, Contrast between his view ('unspeakably remote') and hers ('stood for something real')

passage: Lady Bexborough, she said once, held herself upright (so did Clarissa herself; she never lounged in any sense of the word; she was straight as a dart, a little rigid in fact). She said they had a kind of courage which the older she grew the more she respected.
explanation: Peter recalls Clarissa’s words and interprets her posture as symbolic. The parenthetical comparison shows his continued observation of her physicality, rendered through his evaluative lens in third person with no narratorial intrusion.
evidence: Reported speech attributed to Clarissa filtered through Peter’s memory, Physical description serving as psychological insight ('straight as a dart, a little rigid'), Absence of narratorial commentary—pure transmission of Peter’s thought
notes: Brief but clearly interior; could be seen as omniscient description, but diction ('a little rigid in fact') carries Peter’s judgment.

passage: With twice his wits, she had to see things through his eyes--one of the tragedies of married life. With a mind of her own, she must always be quoting Richard--as if one couldn’t know to a tittle what Richard thought by reading the _Morning Post_ of a morning!
explanation: Peter’s sarcastic tone ('as if one couldn’t know...') and sweeping generalization ('tragedies of married life') reflect his cynical interpretation of Clarissa’s marriage. The narrator conveys this without mediation.
evidence: Irony and sarcasm characteristic of Peter’s voice, Generalized aphorism masking personal grievance, Dismissive reference to newspaper as proxy for Richard’s unoriginality

passage: These parties for example were all for him, or for her idea of him (to do Richard justice he would have been happier farming in Norfolk). She made her drawing-room a sort of meeting-place; she had a genius for it. Over and over again he had seen her take some raw youth, twist him, turn him, wake him up; set him going.
explanation: Peter acknowledges Clarissa’s social talent with grudging respect. The kinetic verbs ('twist him, turn him, wake him up') simulate his visual memory of her in action, rendered through his admiring yet detached perspective.
evidence: Action-oriented verbs conveying dynamic mental image, Qualified praise ('to do Richard justice') showing balanced judgment, Third-person framing with character-specific diction ('genius for it')

passage: Oddly enough, she was one of the most thoroughgoing sceptics he had ever met, and possibly (this was a theory he used to make up to account for her, so transparent in some ways, so inscrutable in others), possibly she said to herself, As we are a doomed race, chained to a sinking ship (her favourite reading as a girl was Huxley and Tyndall, and they were fond of these nautical metaphors), as the whole thing is a bad joke, let us, at any rate, do our part; mitigate the sufferings of our fellow-prisoners (Huxley again); decorate the dungeon with flowers and air-cushions; be as decent as we possibly can.
explanation: Peter constructs an imagined inner monologue for Clarissa, blending his knowledge of her reading with his interpretive theory. This is reverie—constructive fantasy—about her consciousness, not direct recall. The narrator presents it as Peter’s hypothesis.
evidence: Explicit framing as conjecture ('this was a theory he used to make up'), Invented first-person thought attributed to Clarissa, Associative references to her intellectual influences (Huxley, Tyndall)
notes: Clear case of reverie/constructive fantasy layered over indirect interior monologue.

passage: Those ruffians, the Gods, shan’t have it all their own way,--her notion being that the Gods, who never lost a chance of hurting, thwarting and spoiling human lives were seriously put out if, all the same, you behaved like a lady.
explanation: Continuation of Peter’s constructed fantasy of Clarissa’s inner philosophy. The anthropomorphized 'ruffians, the Gods' and the defiant stance reflect his romanticized interpretation of her resilience.
evidence: Colloquial, dramatic diction ('ruffians, the Gods'), Attribution to Clarissa’s supposed worldview ('her notion being'), Emotional tone of resistance matching Peter’s admiration
notes: Part of the same reverie sequence as previous passage.

passage: That phase came directly after Sylvia’s death--that horrible affair. To see your own sister killed by a falling tree (all Justin Parry’s fault--all his carelessness) before your very eyes, a girl too on the verge of life, the most gifted of them, Clarissa always said, was enough to turn one bitter. Later she wasn’t so positive perhaps; she thought there were no Gods; no one was to blame; and so she evolved this atheist’s religion of doing good for the sake of goodness.
explanation: Peter traces the origin of Clarissa’s philosophy to trauma, moving associatively from event to emotional consequence to philosophical adaptation. The parenthetical blame ('all Justin Parry’s fault') shows his absorbed perspective on her past.
evidence: Causal chain linking trauma to worldview, Parenthetical moral judgment reflecting shared history, Speculative phrasing ('Later she wasn’t so positive perhaps') showing interpretive uncertainty

passage: And of course she enjoyed life immensely. It was her nature to enjoy (though goodness only knows, she had her reserves; it was a mere sketch, he often felt, that even he, after all these years, could make of Clarissa). If you walked with her in Hyde Park now it was a bed of tulips, now a child in a perambulator, now some absurd little drama she made up on the spur of the moment. (Very likely, she would have talked to those lovers, if she had thought them unhappy.)
explanation: Peter celebrates Clarissa’s joy in sensory details, listing impressions as if reliving walks with her. The parentheses reveal his intimate speculation about her behavior, all rendered through his affectionate, slightly envious lens.
evidence: Catalog of sensory joys mimicking perceptual flow, Parenthetical speculation showing deep familiarity, Comparative judgment ('none of that sense of moral virtue') revealing personal values

passage: She had a sense of comedy that was really exquisite, but she needed people, always people, to bring it out, with the inevitable result that she frittered her time away, lunching, dining, giving these incessant parties of hers, talking nonsense, sayings things she didn’t mean, blunting the edge of her mind, losing her discrimination.
explanation: Peter’s critique of Clarissa’s social life reveals his ambivalence—admiration for her wit mixed with disappointment at its dissipation. The accumulative list ('lunching, dining...') enacts his sense of waste.
evidence: Accumulative syntax conveying mounting criticism, Value-laden terms ('frittered', 'blunting', 'losing'), Contrast between potential ('exquisite comedy') and practice ('talking nonsense')

passage: There she would sit at the head of the table taking infinite pains with some old buffer who might be useful to Dalloway--they knew the most appalling bores in Europe--or in came Elizabeth and everything must give way to _her_. She was at a High School, at the inarticulate stage last time he was over, a round-eyed, pale-faced girl, with nothing of her mother in her, a silent stolid creature, who took it all as a matter of course, let her mother make a fuss of her, and then said “May I go now?” like a child of four; going off, Clarissa explained, with that mixture of amusement and pride which Dalloway himself seemed to rouse in her, to play hockey.
explanation: Peter’s memory of Elizabeth shifts from social critique to personal observation, marked by his disappointment in her lack of resemblance to Clarissa. The quoted phrase ('May I go now?') and physical description convey his detached, slightly critical perspective.
evidence: Shift from general complaint to specific recalled scene, Direct quotation anchoring memory in sensory detail, Judgmental descriptors ('silent stolid creature', 'nothing of her mother')

passage: And now Elizabeth was “out,” presumably; thought him an old fogy, laughed at her mother’s friends. Ah well, so be it. The compensation of growing old, Peter Walsh thought, coming out of Regent’s Park, and holding his hat in hand, was simply this; that the passions remain as strong as ever, but one has gained--at last!--the power which adds the supreme flavour to existence,--the power of taking hold of experience, of turning it round, slowly, in the light.
explanation: Peter articulates a philosophical consolation for aging. The tactile metaphor ('turning it round, slowly, in the light') simulates reflective calm. Though the narrator mentions Peter by name, the thought content and lyrical phrasing are his alone.
evidence: Metaphor of handling experience as physical object, Rhythmic, measured syntax enacting contemplation, Shift from resignation ('Ah well, so be it') to affirmation
notes: Also functions as simulation of state of mind (serene reflection).

passage: A terrible confession it was (he put his hat on again), but now, at the age of fifty-three one scarcely needed people any more. Life itself, every moment of it, every drop of it, here, this instant, now, in the sun, in Regent’s Park, was enough. Too much indeed. A whole lifetime was too short to bring out, now that one had acquired the power, the full flavour; to extract every ounce of pleasure, every shade of meaning; which both were so much more solid than they used to be, so much less personal.
explanation: Peter’s epiphany about the sufficiency of solitary experience is rendered with sensuous intensity. The repetition ('every moment... every drop... every ounce') and present-tense immediacy simulate joyous absorption in the moment.
evidence: Present-tense immersion in sensory environment ('here, this instant, now, in the sun'), Accumulative, rhythmic phrasing enacting plenitude, Paradoxical claim ('Too much indeed') expressing overwhelming joy
notes: Strong case of simulation of state of mind (happiness/ecstasy) via form.

passage: It was impossible that he should ever suffer again as Clarissa had made him suffer. explanation: Peter contrasts past and present emotional states, moving from self-protective assertion to speculative reversal of roles. The parenthetical prayer reveals his self-consciousness, and the rhetorical question mimics internal debate.
evidence: Temporal contrast structuring emotional reflection, Parenthetical aside showing vulnerability, Role reversal fantasy ('now _she_ was in love with _him_') as self-soothing

passage: And that perhaps was the reason why, when the ship actually sailed, he felt an extraordinary relief, wanted nothing so much as to be alone; was annoyed to find all her little attentions--cigars, notes, a rug for the voyage--in his cabin. Every one if they were honest would say the same; one doesn’t want people after fifty; one doesn’t want to go on telling women they are pretty; that’s what most men of fifty would say, Peter Walsh thought, if they were honest.
explanation: Peter justifies his rejection of Daisy’s affection by universalizing his preference for solitude. The generalization ('Every one if they were honest...') masks personal discomfort as common wisdom.

passage: But then these astonishing accesses of emotion--bursting into tears this morning, what was all that about? explanation: Peter analyzes his emotional outburst with self-reproach. The rhetorical questions and physical gesture (holding knife) simulate his attempt to gain distance from shame. The aphorism about jealousy reveals his self-diagnosis.
evidence: Self-directed rhetorical questions expressing confusion and shame, Physical action as cognitive distancing strategy, Generalized maxim masking personal vulnerability
notes: Simulation of state of mind (shame/anxiety) through fragmented self-interrogation.

passage: She had been meeting Major Orde, Daisy said in her last letter; said it on purpose he knew; said it to make him jealous; he could see her wrinkling her forehead as she wrote, wondering what she could say to hurt him; and yet it made no difference; he was furious! All this pother of coming to England and seeing lawyers wasn’t to marry her, but to prevent her from marrying anybody else. That was what tortured him, that was what came over him when he saw Clarissa so calm, so cold, so intent on her dress or whatever it was; realising what she might have spared him, what she had reduced him to--a whimpering, snivelling old ass.
explanation: Peter’s jealousy erupts in a cascade of accusations and self-abasement. The exclamatory syntax ('he was furious!'), imagined scene (Daisy writing), and self-insult ('whimpering, snivelling old ass') simulate uncontrolled anger.

passage: But women, he thought, shutting his pocket-knife, don’t know what passion is. They don’t know the meaning of it to men. Clarissa was as cold as an icicle. There she would sit on the sofa by his side, let him take her hand, give him one kiss--

evidence: Gendered generalization masking personal hurt, Thermal metaphor conveying emotional distance, Abrupt dash ending simulating interrupted thought/memory

passage: As the ancient song bubbled up opposite Regent’s Park Tube station still the earth seemed green and flowery; still, though it issued from so rude a mouth, a mere hole in the earth, muddy too, matted with root fibres and tangled grasses, still the old bubbling burbling song, soaking through the knotted roots of infinite ages, and skeletons and treasure, streamed away in rivulets over the pavement and all along the Marylebone Road, and down towards Euston, fertilising, leaving a damp stain. Still remembering how once in some primeval May she had walked with her lover, this rusty pump, this battered old woman with one hand exposed for coppers the other clutching her side, would still be there in ten million years, remembering how once she had walked in May, where the sea flows now, with whom it did not matter--he was a man, oh yes, a man who had loved her.

evidence: Geological imagery ('knotted roots of infinite ages') controlled by narrator, Hypothetical future projection ('would still be there in ten million years'), Detached characterization ('with whom it did not matter') showing external perspective

passage: But the passage of ages had blurred the clarity of that ancient May day; the bright petalled flowers were hoar and silver frosted; and she no longer saw, when she implored him (as she did now quite clearly) “look in my eyes with thy sweet eyes intently,” she no longer saw brown eyes, black whiskers or sunburnt face but only a looming shape, a shadow shape, to which, with the bird-like freshness of the very aged she still twittered “give me your hand and let me press it gently” (Peter Walsh couldn’t help giving the poor creature a coin as he stepped into his taxi), “and if some one should see, what matter they?” she demanded; and her fist clutched at her side, and she smiled, pocketing her shilling, and all peering inquisitive eyes seemed blotted out, and the passing generations--the pavement was crowded with bustling middle-class people--vanished, like leaves, to be trodden under, to be soaked and steeped and made mould of by that eternal spring--
explanation: The narrator interweaves the singer’s imagined words with Peter’s action and the crowd’s presence, creating a cinematic montage. However, all perspectives are filtered through the narrator’s poetic vision, not rendered from within any single consciousness.
evidence: Intercutting of singer’s speech, Peter’s action, and crowd description, Narrator-controlled metaphors ('vanished, like leaves... made mould'), External observation of physical gestures ('fist clutched', 'smiled, pocketing')
notes: Space-montage structure, but all mediated by omniscient narrator.

passage: “Poor old woman,” said Rezia Warren Smith, waiting to cross. Oh poor old wretch! Suppose it was a wet night? Suppose one’s father, or somebody who had known one in better days had happened to pass, and saw one standing there in the gutter? And where did she sleep at night? Cheerfully, almost gaily, the invincible thread of sound wound up into the air like the smoke from a cottage chimney, winding up clean beech trees and issuing in a tuft of blue smoke among the topmost leaves. “And if some one should see, what matter they?”
explanation: Rezia’s empathetic response to the singer is rendered in third person with her emotional logic. The rhetorical questions and sudden shift to lyrical description of the song reflect her hopeful interpretation, triggered by her own anxiety.
evidence: Empathetic hypotheticals ('Suppose it was a wet night?'), Shift from concern to poetic imagery mirroring emotional uplift, Quoted refrain connecting singer’s words to Rezia’s hope

passage: Since she was so unhappy, for weeks and weeks now, Rezia had given meanings to things that happened, almost felt sometimes that she must stop people in the street, if they looked good, kind people, just to say to them “I am unhappy”; and this old woman singing in the street “if some one should see, what matter they?” made her suddenly quite sure that everything was going to be right. They were going to Sir William Bradshaw; she thought his name sounded nice; he would cure Septimus at once.
explanation: Rezia’s desperate hope is conveyed through her attribution of meaning to the singer’s words. The associative leap from despair to certainty ('made her suddenly quite sure') reveals her fragile optimism, rendered through her perspective in third person.
evidence: Confessional impulse ('just to say to them “I am unhappy”'), Magical thinking linking external event to personal salvation, Childlike trust in authority ('he would cure Septimus at once')

passage: Something was up, Mr. Brewer knew; Mr. Brewer, managing clerk at Sibleys and Arrowsmiths, auctioneers, valuers, land and estate agents; something was up, he thought, and, being paternal with his young men, and thinking very highly of Smith’s abilities, and prophesying that he would, in ten or fifteen years, succeed to the leather arm-chair in the inner room under the skylight with the deed-boxes round him, “if he keeps his health,” said Mr. Brewer, and that was the danger--he looked weakly; advised football, invited him to supper and was seeing his way to consider recommending a rise of salary
explanation: The passage renders Mr. Brewer's thoughts using third-person pronouns but adopts his diction and concerns (e.g., 'something was up,' 'if he keeps his health'). The narrator is present but yields to Mr. Brewer’s consciousness, characteristic of indirect interior monologue.
evidence: Third-person reference ('he thought') paired with character-specific idiom, Content reflects Mr. Brewer’s professional and paternal perspective, Light narratorial framing with embedded free indirect thought

passage: Septimus was one of the first to volunteer. He went to France to save an England which consisted almost entirely of Shakespeare’s plays and Miss Isabel Pole in a green dress walking in a square.
explanation: The narrator describes Septimus’s motivation from an external perspective, interpreting his idealized vision of England. The imagery ('Shakespeare’s plays,' 'green dress') conveys his subjective perception, but the narrator retains full authority.
evidence: Narrator explicitly interprets Septimus’s inner motive, Use of conventional third-person syntax and structure, Sensory imagery filtered through narrator’s description of consciousness

passage: It was a case of two dogs playing on a hearth-rug; one worrying a paper screw, snarling, snapping, giving a pinch, now and then, at the old dog’s ear; the other lying somnolent, blinking at the fire, raising a paw, turning and growling good-temperedly.
explanation: This extended metaphor renders Septimus’s perception of his relationship with Evans through vivid, sustained imagery. Though narrated externally, it expresses his subjective emotional understanding of their bond.
evidence: Dense, metaphorical sensory language depicting relational dynamics, Blurring of external reality and internal perception, Imagery serves as the primary vehicle for conveying consciousness

passage: For now that it was all over, truce signed, and the dead buried, he had, especially in the evening, these sudden thunder-claps of fear. He could not feel. As he opened the door of the room where the Italian girls sat making hats, he could see them; could hear them; they were rubbing wires among coloured beads in saucers; they were turning buckram shapes this way and that; the table was all strewn with feathers, spangles, silks, ribbons; scissors were rapping on the table; but something failed him; he could not feel.
explanation: The narrator describes Septimus’s dissociative state, using rich sensory detail to simulate his inability to connect emotionally. Though narrated externally, the passage enacts his psychological distress through rhythmic accumulation of sensory data followed by emotional void.
evidence: Narrator reports mental state ('he could not feel'), Sensory overload contrasts with emotional numbness, Rhythm and density mimic affective disconnection
notes: Functions as both omniscient description and simulation of anxiety through form.

passage: Even taste (Rezia liked ices, chocolates, sweet things) had no relish to him. He put down his cup on the little marble table. He looked at people outside; happy they seemed, collecting in the middle of the street, shouting, laughing, squabbling over nothing. But he could not taste, he could not feel. In the tea-shop among the tables and the chattering waiters the appalling fear came over him--he could not feel. He could reason; he could read, Dante for example, quite easily (“Septimus, do put down your book,” said Rezia, gently shutting the _Inferno_), he could add up his bill; his brain was perfect; it must be the fault of the world then--that he could not feel.
explanation: The passage blends third-person narration with Septimus’s internal logic and diction ('it must be the fault of the world'). The repetitive structure and contrast between cognitive function and emotional paralysis simulate despair.
evidence: Shift from narrator to character’s reasoning pattern, Repetition of 'he could not feel' mirrors obsessive thought, Syntax enacts emotional flatness amid sensory detail

passage: One cannot bring children into a world like this. One cannot perpetuate suffering, or increase the breed of these lustful animals, who have no lasting emotions, but only whims and vanities, eddying them now this way, now that.
explanation: This first-person-like thought appears without narratorial mediation or reporting verbs. It presents Septimus’s raw, unfiltered judgment about humanity and procreation, characteristic of direct interior monologue.
evidence: No narrator or reporting clause introduces the thought, First-person perspective implied through 'One cannot', Philosophical yet fragmented, mimicking pre-speech thought
notes: Though phrased impersonally ('One cannot'), the thought is clearly Septimus’s and lacks any narratorial frame.

passage: There was Brewer at the office, with his waxed moustache, coral tie-pin, white slip, and pleasurable emotions--all coldness and clamminess within,--his geraniums ruined in the War--his cook’s nerves destroyed; or Amelia What’shername, handing round cups of tea punctually at five--a leering, sneering obscene little harpy; and the Toms and Berties in their starched shirt fronts oozing thick drops of vice.

passage: Far away he heard her sobbing; he heard it accurately, he noticed it distinctly; he compared it to a piston thumping. But he felt nothing. evidence: Third-person pronouns with character-anchored perception, Metaphor enacts emotional detachment, Rhythmic repetition emphasizes emotional void

passage: So there was no excuse; nothing whatever the matter, except the sin for which human nature had condemned him to death; that he did not feel. explanation: This passage immerses the reader in Septimus’s self-accusatory spiral. No narrator mediates; the thought flows associatively from guilt over Evans to broader moral collapse, with hallucinatory imagery enacting shame.
evidence: No narratorial intrusion or reporting verbs, Associative chain of self-reproach, Personified 'crimes' and bodily metaphors simulate psychological torment

passage: Once you stumble, Septimus wrote on the back of a postcard, human nature is on you. Holmes is on you.

passage: But even Holmes himself could not touch this last relic straying on the edge of the world, this outcast, who gazed back at the inhabited regions, who lay, like a drowned sailor, on the shore of the world.

explanation: Though this section conveys Rezia’s intuition ('Rezia Warren Smith divined it'), the passage itself is narrated externally with full authorial control. The narrator interprets and elaborates on Lady Bradshaw’s subjugation using metaphorical language that belongs to the narrator, not Rezia.
evidence: Narrator uses psycho-narration ('divined it'), Extended metaphor ('slow sinking, water-logged') is narratorial, Third-person omniscient perspective dominates
notes: While anchored in Rezia’s perception, the passage is primarily omniscient description of consciousness rather than immersion in it. The narrator constructs the insight, not Rezia.

passage: So he ruminated. It was his habit. He did not go deeply. He brushed surfaces; the dead languages, the living, life in Constantinople, Paris, Rome; riding, shooting, tennis, it had been once.
explanation: The narrator describes Hugh’s mental habits from outside, summarizing his superficiality and pastimes. Though it touches on his consciousness ('ruminated', 'habit'), it does so through conventional third-person narration with interpretive commentary.
evidence: Narrator explicitly states 'It was his habit', Summarizing tone ('He did not go deeply'), List of experiences framed by narrator, not Hugh

passage: Which was she now, the General’s great-grand-daughter? great-great-grand-daughter? Richard Dalloway asked himself.
explanation: The passage renders Richard's unspoken thought directly, using third-person framing ('asked himself') but with syntax and diction that reflect his internal questioning. The lack of quotation marks and the introspective, fragmented phrasing indicate indirect interior monologue.
evidence: Third-person framing with 'asked himself', Fragmented, self-posed genealogical question mimicking thought rhythm

passage: Better wait to put before them the question that bothered her (about making an appeal to the public; if so, in what terms and so on), better wait until they have had their coffee, Lady Bruton thought;
explanation: Lady Bruton's strategic deliberation is rendered in her own idiom ('bothered her', 'better wait') within a third-person frame marked by 'thought'. The parenthetical insertion mimics the associative flow of planning thought.
evidence: Explicit 'thought' tag with character-anchored diction, Parenthetical clause reflecting real-time mental processing

passage: Milly Brush saw that; saw a depth in the brown of his eyes; saw him hesitate; consider; which interested her, as Mr. Dalloway always interested her, for what was he thinking, she wondered, about Peter Walsh? That Peter Walsh had been in love with Clarissa; that he would go back directly after lunch and find Clarissa; that he would tell her, in so many words, that he loved her. Yes, he would say that.
explanation: Milly's speculative chain of thoughts about Richard's mind is rendered through third-person narration with minimal mediation. The repetitive 'that...' structure and conclusive 'Yes, he would say that' mimic her internal certainty-building process.
evidence: Speculative sequence introduced by 'she wondered', Anaphoric 'that...' clauses enacting mental projection

passage: Hugh was very slow, Lady Bruton thought. He was getting fat, she noticed. Richard always kept himself in the pink of condition. She was getting impatient; the whole of her being was setting positively, undeniably, domineeringly brushing aside all this unnecessary trifling (Peter Walsh and his affairs) upon that subject which engaged her attention, and not merely her attention, but that fibre which was the ramrod of her soul...
explanation: Lady Bruton's mounting impatience is rendered through her evaluative judgments ('very slow', 'getting fat') and visceral metaphor ('ramrod of her soul'). The narrator uses her emphatic adverbs ('positively, undeniably, domineeringly') to channel her consciousness.
evidence: Character-specific judgments framed by 'thought' and 'noticed', Bodily metaphor ('ramrod of her soul') expressing subjective urgency

passage: Emigration had become, in short, largely Lady Bruton. But she had to write. And one letter to the _Times_, she used to say to Miss Brush, cost her more than to organise an expedition to South Africa (which she had done in the war). After a morning’s battle beginning, tearing up, beginning again, she used to feel the futility of her own womanhood as she felt it on no other occasion...
explanation: The narrator describes Lady Bruton's emotional state ('futility of her own womanhood') from an external perspective, interpreting her experience rather than rendering raw thought. The metaphorical language ('morning’s battle') belongs to the narrator’s descriptive framework.
evidence: Narrator’s interpretive phrasing ('used to feel the futility'), Metaphorical description of writing process as 'battle'

passage: Always she went back to those fields down in Devonshire, where she had jumped the brooks on Patty, her pony, with Mortimer and Tom, her brothers. And there were the dogs; there were the rats; there were her father and mother on the lawn under the trees, with the tea-things out, and the beds of dahlias, the hollyhocks, the pampas grass; and they, little wretches, always up to some mischief! stealing back through the shrubbery, so as not to be seen, all bedraggled from some roguery.
explanation: Lady Bruton constructs a sustained nostalgic scene blending sensory details (dahlias, hollyhocks) with childhood actions. The present-tense immediacy ('stealing back') and invented dialogue ('little wretches') mark this as constructive fantasy rather than mere memory recall.
evidence: Sensory-rich invented scene with present-tense verbs, Childhood perspective enacted through exclamatory phrasing

passage: And they went further and further from her, being attached to her by a thin thread (since they had lunched with her) which would stretch and stretch, get thinner and thinner as they walked across London; as if one’s friends were attached to one’s body, after lunching with them, by a thin thread, which (as she dozed there) became hazy with the sound of bells, striking the hour or ringing to service, as a single spider’s thread is blotted with rain-drops, and, burdened, sags down.
explanation: The passage immerses us directly in Lady Bruton’s drowsy consciousness without narratorial mediation. The extended spider-thread metaphor enacts her fading connection to friends, with syntactic elongation ('stretch and stretch... thinner and thinner') mirroring mental dissolution.
evidence: No narrator tags or third-person framing, Metaphor structurally enacting emotional state (thread thinning/sagging)

passage: Life had thrown up this wreckage; shop windows full of coloured paste, and one stood stark with the lethargy of the old, stiff with the rigidity of the old, looking in.
explanation: Richard’s bleak perception of consumerism as 'wreckage' emerges without narratorial mediation. The shift to impersonal 'one' reflects his dissociative state, while 'lethargy of the old' conveys embodied despair through compressed imagery.
evidence: Unmediated first-person perspective via impersonal 'one', Visceral metaphor ('lethargy of the old') expressing existential fatigue

passage: The worthlessness of this life did strike Richard pretty forcibly--buying necklaces for Evelyn. If he’d had a boy he’d have said, Work, work. But he had his Elizabeth; he adored his Elizabeth.
explanation: Richard’s abrupt value judgment ('worthlessness') and conditional fantasy ('If he’d had a boy') emerge without narratorial framing. The imperative repetition ('Work, work') and pivot to Elizabeth reveal raw emotional recalibration.
evidence: Unmediated value judgment with em-dash interruption, Conditional construction revealing private longing

passage: He wanted to open the drawing-room door and come in holding out something; a present for Clarissa. Only what? But Hugh was on his legs again. He was unspeakably pompous. Really, after dealing here for thirty-five years he was not going to be put off by a mere boy who did not know his business.
explanation: Richard’s thwarted desire ('Only what?') abruptly shifts to contempt for Hugh through associative irritation. The lack of transition markers and colloquial phrasing ('unspeakably pompous') render unfiltered thought.
evidence: Abrupt topic shift from Clarissa to Hugh, Colloquial intensifier ('unspeakably') expressing spontaneous judgment

passage: Why not? Really it was a miracle thinking of the war, and thousands of poor chaps, with all their lives before them, shovelled together, already half forgotten; it was a miracle. Here he was walking across London to say to Clarissa in so many words that he loved her. Which one never does say, he thought. Partly one’s lazy; partly one’s shy.
explanation: Richard’s epiphany about love’s rarity unfolds through rhythmic repetition ('it was a miracle') and self-interrogation ('Why not?'). The syntax enacts wonder through parallel clauses and present-tense immediacy, formally simulating awe.
evidence: Rhetorical question initiating emotional revelation, Repetitive structure ('it was a miracle') enacting wonder

passage: The time comes when it can’t be said; one’s too shy to say it, he thought, pocketing his sixpence or two of change, setting off with his great bunch held against his body to Westminster to say straight out in so many words (whatever she might think of him), holding out his flowers, “I love you.” Why not? Really it was a miracle thinking of the war, and thousands of poor chaps, with all their lives before them, shovelled together, already half forgotten; it was a miracle. Here he was walking across London to say to Clarissa in so many words that he loved her. Which one never does say, he thought. Partly one’s lazy; partly one’s shy. And Clarissa--it was difficult to think of her; except in starts, as at luncheon, when he saw her quite distinctly; their whole life.
explanation: This passage renders Richard Dalloway’s unspoken thoughts through third-person narration with minimal narratorial intrusion. The diction ('one’s too shy', 'Why not?', 'Partly one’s lazy') reflects his internal voice, while reporting verbs like 'he thought' are light and parenthetical. The associative flow—from personal hesitation to wartime reflection to Clarissa—shows free association triggered by his emotional state.
evidence: Third-person pronouns with character-anchored diction ('one’s too shy'), Light use of 'he thought' without interpretive commentary, Associative shift from personal emotion to war memory to Clarissa

passage: He stopped at the crossing; and repeated--being simple by nature, and undebauched, because he had tramped, and shot; being pertinacious and dogged, having championed the down-trodden and followed his instincts in the House of Commons; being preserved in his simplicity yet at the same time grown rather speechless, rather stiff--he repeated that it was a miracle that he should have married Clarissa; a miracle--his life had been a miracle, he thought; hesitating to cross.
explanation: The narrator fully controls this passage, describing Richard’s self-perception using conventional third-person syntax and psycho-narration ('he repeated', 'he thought'). The clauses beginning 'being...' are the narrator’s interpretive gloss on Richard’s character, not his raw thought. Though the content is interior, the technique is external description.
evidence: Narrator provides explanatory clauses ('because he had tramped...'), Use of 'he repeated' and 'he thought' as framing devices, Syntax is grammatically complete and narratively organized

passage: But it did make his blood boil to see little creatures of five or six crossing Piccadilly alone. The police ought to have stopped the traffic at once. He had no illusions about the London police. Indeed, he was collecting evidence of their malpractices; and those costermongers, not allowed to stand their barrows in the streets; and prostitutes, good Lord, the fault wasn’t in them, nor in young men either, but in our detestable social system and so forth; all of which he considered, could be seen considering, grey, dogged, dapper, clean, as he walked across the Park to tell his wife that he loved her.
explanation: Richard’s reformist thoughts are rendered in his own idiom ('good Lord', 'our detestable social system') within third-person narration. The rapid associative chain—from child safety to police to costermongers to prostitutes—follows emotional and ideological resonance rather than logic. The phrase 'all of which he considered' lightly frames the interiority without mediating interpretation.
evidence: Colloquial interjections ('good Lord') reflect character voice, Associative progression across social issues, Minimal narratorial presence beyond 'he considered'

passage: For he would say it in so many words, when he came into the room. Because it is a thousand pities never to say what one feels, he thought, crossing the Green Park and observing with pleasure how in the shade of the trees whole families, poor families, were sprawling; children kicking up their legs; sucking milk; paper bags thrown about, which could easily be picked up (if people objected) by one of those fat gentlemen in livery; for he was of opinion that every park, and every square, during the summer months should be open to children (the grass of the park flushed and faded, lighting up the poor mothers of Westminster and their crawling babies, as if a yellow lamp were moved beneath).
explanation: Richard’s egalitarian sentiment ('a thousand pities never to say what one feels') blends with his perception of park life. The sensory imagery of children and mothers is filtered through his subjective viewpoint ('observing with pleasure'), and the parenthetical simile ('as if a yellow lamp...') reflects his imaginative perception. The narrator remains minimally present via 'he thought'.
evidence: Character-anchored moral assertion ('a thousand pities...'), Sensory details tied to his emotional response ('observing with pleasure'), Parenthetical simile expressing subjective perception

passage: But what could be done for female vagrants like that poor creature, stretched on her elbow (as if she had flung herself on the earth, rid of all ties, to observe curiously, to speculate boldly, to consider the whys and the wherefores, impudent, loose-lipped, humorous), he did not know.
explanation: Richard’s compassionate uncertainty is conveyed through his imagined reconstruction of the vagrant’s posture and mindset. The parenthetical description uses vivid, character-specific diction ('impudent, loose-lipped, humorous') that reflects his attempt to understand her, framed only by 'he did not know' without narratorial interpretation.
evidence: Parenthetical clause renders his speculative perception, Descriptive adjectives reflect his interpretive lens, Framed by minimal reporting verb 'he did not know'

passage: Bearing his flowers like a weapon, Richard Dalloway approached her; intent he passed her; still there was time for a spark between them--she laughed at the sight of him, he smiled good-humouredly, considering the problem of the female vagrant; not that they would ever speak. But he would tell Clarissa that he loved her, in so many words.
explanation: The passage conveys Richard’s internal resolution ('But he would tell Clarissa...') alongside his momentary social interaction. The contrast between the fleeting 'spark' with the vagrant and his commitment to Clarissa is rendered in his own evaluative terms, with only light narratorial framing.
evidence: Internal resolve expressed in character’s phrasing ('in so many words'), Contrast between external encounter and private intention, No narratorial commentary on his feelings
notes: Blends observed action with interior resolve; the boundary between narration and thought is thin but the evaluative tone ('still there was time', 'not that they would ever speak') suggests interior perspective.

passage: He had, once upon a time, been jealous of Peter Walsh; jealous of him and Clarissa. But she had often said to him that she had been right not to marry Peter Walsh; which, knowing Clarissa, was obviously true; she wanted support. Not that she was weak; but she wanted support.
explanation: Richard’s retrospective justification of Clarissa’s choice uses repetitive, self-reassuring phrasing ('Not that she was weak; but she wanted support') characteristic of internal monologue. The clause 'knowing Clarissa, was obviously true' reflects his subjective certainty, rendered without narratorial mediation beyond third-person framing.
evidence: Repetitive, self-justifying structure, Subjective certainty ('obviously true'), Absence of narratorial interpretation

passage: As for Buckingham Palace (like an old prima donna facing the audience all in white) you can’t deny it a certain dignity, he considered, nor despise what does, after all, stand to millions of people (a little crowd was waiting at the gate to see the King drive out) for a symbol, absurd though it is; a child with a box of bricks could have done better, he thought; looking at the memorial to Queen Victoria (whom he could remember in her horn spectacles driving through Kensington), its white mound, its billowing motherliness; but he liked being ruled by the descendant of Horsa; he liked continuity; and the sense of handing on the traditions of the past.
explanation: Richard’s ambivalent reflections on monarchy blend aesthetic judgment ('like an old prima donna'), historical memory (Queen Victoria), and ideological preference ('liked continuity'). The associative movement—from palace to memorial to ancestral myth—is rendered in his evaluative voice, with only 'he considered' and 'he thought' as light narratorial markers.
evidence: Mixed metaphor and historical reference in character’s voice, Associative chain linking visual perception to ideology, Colloquial concession ('absurd though it is') reflecting internal debate

passage: It was a great age in which to have lived. Indeed, his own life was a miracle; let him make no mistake about it; here he was, in the prime of life, walking to his house in Westminster to tell Clarissa that he loved her. Happiness is this he thought.
explanation: Richard’s affirmation of happiness uses rhythmic, declarative syntax ('Happiness is this') that enacts contentment. The repetition of 'miracle' and present-tense immediacy simulate joyful self-assurance. Though framed by 'he thought', the passage’s form mirrors the affective state, qualifying as both indirect interior monologue and simulation of happiness.
evidence: Declarative, rhythmic phrasing enacting joy, Present-tense immediacy ('here he was'), Repetition of 'miracle' reinforcing emotional conviction
notes: Functions as both indirect interior monologue and simulation of state of mind (happiness).

passage: Lunch parties waste the entire afternoon, he thought, approaching his door.
